WHO Response: The WHO's work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: "Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages."

translations.source: Izinjongo zazisa amaphrojekthi nezinqubomgomo kuyo yonke iNhlangano, okuhlanganisa imihlahlandlela mayelana nezindlu, ukuthuthukiswa kwamadolobha, izinhlelo zokuthutha kanye nokulethwa kokunakekelwa kwezempilo. Isibonelo, imihlahlandlela yeWHO Yezindlu kanye nezempilo, eyanyatheliswa okokuqala ngo-2018, iqokomisa umthelela okhulayo wezimo zezindlu empilweni yabantu ngenxa yokukhula kwedolobha, isimo sezulu kanye nezinguquko zabantu. Imihlahlandlela ihlinzeka Amazwe Angamalungu, ozakwethu kanye nomphakathi izincomo ezisekelwe ebufakazini ezifanele ngendawo yokuhlala enganele (ukugcwala), izinga lokushisa eliphansi neliphezulu lasendlini, izingozi zokulimala ekhaya, kanye nokufinyeleleka kwezindlu zabantu abanokukhubazeka kokusebenza. I-Urban Health Initiative (UHI) iphinde igxile ezindleleni zamadolobha zokwenza impilo enhle ngokugqugquzela isenzo esisebenzayo ekungcoleni komoya wasemadolobheni kanye nokungcola kwesimo sezulu esiphila isikhathi esifushane okuyishort-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—ukusindisa izimpilo ngokuxhumanisa impilo, imvelo nentuthuko esimeme. IWHO iphinde ibambisane neClimate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) emkhankasweni weBreathe Life, ohlose ukunqamula ukungcoliswa komoya kanye nokunciphisa ukufa kwabantu ngaphambi kwesikhathi abayizigidi eziyi-7 minyaka yonke okubangwayo.
translations.reference: The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery. For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.hypothesis: The objectives encompass projects and policies across all sectors, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation, and healthcare provision. For example, the WHO's Guidelines on Housing and Health, first published in 2018, address the growing impact of housing conditions on people's health due to urban growth, climate change, and demographic shifts. These guidelines provide Member States, partners, and the public with evidence-based recommendations for creating healthy and sustainable living environments, including adequate housing, affordable heating and cooling, safety from hazards, and access to safe and affordable transportation. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) focuses on urban strategies for improving health by promoting clean air and reducing exposure to short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—harmful air pollutants that have a short lifespan in the atmosphere and contribute to climate change. The WHO is also collaborating with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) in the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to reduce air pollution and save millions of lives each year.

translations.source: Ugcunsula Uhlolojikelele: I-syphilis isifo esithathelwana ngocansi segciwane (STI) esibangelwa i-Treponema pallidum esiholela ekuguleni okukhulu nokufa, futhi siyelapheka. ISyphilis isakazwa ngokuthintana ngokocansi nezilonda ezithathelwanayo, ngokumpontshelwa igazi noma isuka kowesifazane okhulelwe iye embungwini wakhe. Ukusulelana ngogcunsula kusuka kumama kuya enganeni kuvame ukulimaza umbungu ezimeni ezingazange zibonwe noma zingelashwe ngokwanele ekuqaleni kokukhulelwa. Isyphilis kamama, lapho ingelashwa, yelashwa sekwephuzile noma ingelashwa ngepenicillin, iphumela emiphumeleni yokuzalwa engemihle i-adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) elinganiselwa ku-50−80% wezigameko, kuye ngesigaba sogcunsula.
translations.reference: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.
translations.hypothesis: General Overview: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum, which can lead to serious health problems and death. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with an infected person, through sharing needles, or from a mother to her child during pregnancy. Congenital syphilis, which occurs when a mother contracts syphilis during pregnancy and passes it on to her baby, can result in severe health problems for the child, including developmental delays, blindness, and death.

translations.source: Lawa ma-ABO avame ukuba nzima futhi ahlanganisa ukubeletha (ikakhulukazi), ukufa kwezingane ezisanda kuzalwa, ngaphambi kwesikhathi, ukuzalwa okunesisindo esiphansi, kanye nezinsana ezizalwa nazo. ISyphilis ingavinjelwa ngokusebenzisa izinqubo zocansi eziphephile ezihlanganisa ukusetshenziswa okufanele nokungaguquguquki kwamakhondomu. Abantu abaningi abanogcunsula abanazo izimpawu noma banezimpawu ezincane kuphela ezinganakwa. Ukuhlonza ukutheleleka ngokuhlolwa kwaselabhorethri kanye nokwelashwa kwamacala amahle kuzovimbela ukudluliselwa okuqhubekayo kanye nemiphumela yokukhulelwa engemihle kuhlanganise nogcunsula wokuzalwa. Ukuhlolwa kungenziwa kusetshenziswa ukuhlola okusheshayo okunganikeza imiphumela ngaphansi kwemizuzu engama-20, okuvumela ukwelashwa ngokushesha.
translations.reference: These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.hypothesis: These STIs are often asymptomatic and can include bleeding, pain (especially in the genitals), and miscarriage, stillbirth, or premature birth. Syphilis can be prevented through safe sex practices, including consistent and correct condom use. Many people with syphilis have no symptoms or only mild symptoms that may go unnoticed. Diagnosis can be made through laboratory testing and appropriate treatment can prevent further transmission and long-term health problems, including congenital syphilis. Testing can be done using a rapid test that can provide results in under 20 minutes, allowing for prompt treatment.

translations.source: Uma ungelashwa, ugcunsula uhlala iminyaka eminingi futhi udlula ezigabeni ezihlukene. Ukwelashwa kwe-syphilis kwenziwa nge-penicillin yomjovo i-benzathine, ngemithamo engu-1 kuya kwemi-3 kuye ngesigaba sesifo. Ipenicillin kuphela engavimbela ugcunsula wokuzalwa. Ukwelashwa nge-penicillin kuye kwaphazanyiswa ukushoda ngezikhathi ezithile kanye nesikhathi eside kwebenzathine penicillin emazweni amaningi, kuhlanganise namazwe anemali engenayo ephezulu, ephansi naphakathi. Izimpawu: Abantu abaningi abanogcunsula abazibonisi izimpawu noma abaziboni.
translations.reference: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them.
translations.hypothesis: If left untreated, syphilis can cause serious health problems and even death. Syphilis is treated with penicillin, typically in three doses, depending on the stage of the infection. Penicillin is effective in preventing congenital syphilis. However, treatment with penicillin has been disrupted in some countries due to shortages and the high cost of the drug. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not experience symptoms, or their symptoms are mild.

translations.source: Uma ungalashwa, ugcunsula uthatha iminyaka eminingi futhi ubonakala ngezigaba ezahlukene. Ukusulelana ngokocansi kuvame ukuvela ezigabeni zokuqala, okungukuthi, kuze kufike eminyakeni emi-2 ngemva kokutheleleka. Esigabeni sokuqala (ugcunsula oyinhloko), esihlala ngokwesilinganiso izinsuku ezingama-21, isilonda esisodwa, esingenabuhlungu, ngokuvamile esiqinile nesiyindilinga (chancre) sivela endaweni yokujova (ukuthintwa), ngokuvamile esithweni sangasese, epipini noma endunu kungase futhi kube ngaphandle kwesitho sobulili) futhi kungase kungabonakali. Ichancre yelapheka ngokwelashwa noma ngaphandle kokwelashwa phakathi kwamaviki ama-3 kuya kwayi-10. Uma singelashwa, lesi sifo sidlulela esigabeni sesibili.
translations.reference: Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.hypothesis: If left untreated, the wart can take years to heal and may appear as multiple, different-sized growths. Wart removal typically takes several months and may involve multiple treatments. In the first stage (initial wart), which lasts for approximately 21 days, a single, painless, firm, and well-defined (crater-like) growth appears at the site of the injury, usually on the sole of the foot, between the toes, or on the sole of the hand. The crater-like wart heals within 3 to 10 weeks. If left untreated, the wart progresses to the second stage.

translations.source: Isyphilis yesibili ibonakala ngokuqubuka okungahlukahluka kakhulu futhi kulingise ezinye izimo ezithathelwanayo noma ezingatheleleki, kodwa ngokuphawulekayo kuthinta izintende nezinyawo. Ukuqubuka akulume futhi kungaba kuncane ngokwanele ukuba kunganakwa. Ezindaweni ezifudumele nezimanzi zomzimba, njenge-anus kanye ne-labia, izilonda ezinkulu ezimhlophe noma ezimpunga eziphakanyisiwe zingase zikhule endaweni yechancre eyinhloko (condyloma lata). Izimpawu nezimpawu zesyphilis yesibili ziyazixazulula ngokuzenzekelayo, ngisho nangaphandle kokwelashwa. Ngaphandle kokwelashwa, isigaba esicashile siqala.
translations.reference: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts.
translations.hypothesis: Secondary syphilis presents with a wide range of symptoms, including skin lesions and mucous membrane infections, but it is particularly noticeable in the genital area. The lesions can be small or large, and may be painless. In warm, moist areas such as the anus and labia, large, white or grayish patches may develop (condyloma lata). The lesions and symptoms of secondary syphilis typically resolve on their own, even without treatment. However, if left untreated, the condition can become chronic.

translations.source: Isyphilis equbisile ayivezi zimpawu zomtholampilo noma izimpawu futhi ukwelashwa kuhlukile kwesigaba sokuqala. Ngemva kweminyaka noma amashumi eminyaka ngaphandle kokwelashwa, izifo zezinzwa nenhliziyo phakathi kwabanye zingase zenzeke. Ukwelashwa: Ukuxilongwa kwesyphilis kusekelwe emlandweni womuntu, ukuhlolwa komzimba, ukuhlolwa kwaselabhorethri futhi ngezinye izikhathi iradiology, njengoba izimpawu zingavamile noma zingabonakali. Ukuhlonza ukutheleleka nge-asymptomatic ngokusebenzisa izivivinyo zaselabhorethri kanye nokwelashwa kwamacala amahle kuzovimbela ukudluliselwa okuqhubekayo kanye nemiphumela yokukhulelwa engemihle, kuhlanganise necongenital syphilis. Ukuhlolwa kwaselabhorethri kwesyphilis kufaka phakathi ukutholwa okuqondile kwe-T. pallidum noma izindlela ezingaqondile njengeserology.
translations.reference: Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.hypothesis: Tertiary syphilis does not present with typical symptoms or signs, and treatment is often complex. After many years or decades without treatment, complications affecting the nervous system and heart may develop. Treatment: Diagnosis of tertiary syphilis is based on clinical findings, physical examination, laboratory tests, and sometimes radiology, as the signs can be atypical or subtle. Treatment typically involves a combination of penicillin injections and supportive care. Early diagnosis and treatment of asymptomatic syphilis using laboratory tests and appropriate medications can prevent further transmission and long-term complications, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory testing for syphilis includes both direct and indirect methods, such as serology.

translations.source: Ukuhlolwa okusheshayo nokuhlolwa kokuxilonga nakho kuyatholakala futhi kunganikeza imiphumela ngaphansi kwemizuzu engama-20, kube lula ukuqaliswa kokwelashwa ngokushesha. IWHO incoma ukuthi ugcunsula welashwe ngomjovo wepenicillin (benzathine penicillin G) ngomthamo owodwa noma emi-3, kuye ngesigaba sesifo. IWHO ineziqondiso ezinemininingwane yokwelapha ugcunsula, kanye neyokuhlolelwa kanye nokwelashwa kogcunsula ekukhulelweni. ICongenital syphilis Okwamanje azikho izivivinyo zokuxilonga ze-syphilis yokuzalwa.
translations.reference: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis.
translations.hypothesis: Rapid screening and testing are available and can provide results in under 20 minutes, making it easier to initiate prompt treatment. The WHO recommends that congenital syphilis be treated with penicillin G, either as a single dose or in divided doses, depending on the severity of the infection. The WHO provides detailed guidelines for the diagnosis, treatment, and management of congenital syphilis. Currently, there are no routine screening tests for congenital syphilis.

translations.source: Zonke izinsana eziphilayo noma ezizalwa sezishonile zabesifazane abanogcunsula kufanele zihlolwe ukuze kutholwe ubufakazi bogcunsula abazalwa nabo. Izingane ezizalwa ziphila kufanele zihlolwe futhi zihlolwe lapho zizalwa ukuze kuchazwe isidingo sokwelashwa. Uma kukhonjisiwe, futhi ngezikhathi zanyanga zonke izinyanga ezingu-3 kuze kube yilapho i-serology yosana, futhi ihlala, ingalungile, njengoba amasosha omzimba kamama angadluliselwa kumntwana ngesikhathi sokukhulelwa.
translations.reference: All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.hypothesis: All infants and young children who have been exposed to HIV should be tested to determine their HIV status. Infants born to mothers with HIV should also be tested at birth to determine if they are infected. If an infant is found to be infected, and if the mother's HIV status is unknown, testing of the mother should be performed during pregnancy, and continued throughout the infant's life.

translations.source: ITaeniasis kanye ne-cysticercosis Uhlolojikelele: Ukutheleleka nge-tapeworm i-Taenia solium kungabangela izimo ezimbili ezihlukene: itaeniasis kanye necysticercosis. ITaeniasis isifo samathumbu esinezikelemu futhi kwenzeka lapho umuntu edla ingulube eluhlaza noma engaphekiwe kahle, enegciwane. ITaeniasis ayinawo umthelela omkhulu empilweni yomuntu. Kodwa ke, amaqanda esikelemu adlula endle yalowo ophethe izikelemu futhi uma kungekho ukukhucululwa kwendle okufanele kungangcolisa imvelo.
translations.reference: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment.
translations.hypothesis: Taeniasis and Cysticercosis: Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can lead to two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is a parasitic infection of the intestines that occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked meat containing the larvae of the tapeworm. Taeniasis does not typically cause serious health problems. However, the larvae can migrate to other parts of the body, such as the brain, and form cysts.

translations.source: Nakuba amaqanda e-tapeworm ehlasela izingulube, amaqanda e-T. solium angase atheleleke kubantu uma emukelwe ngumuntu (ngomzila we-fecal-oral, noma ngokudla ukudla noma amanzi angcolile). Ukungenwa amaqanda ezikelemu kubangela ukutheleleka nge-parasite yezibungu ezicutshini ezinjengezicubu, isikhumba, amehlo kanye nesimiso sezinzwa esimaphakathi (human cysticercosis), okungaba nemiphumela elimazayo empilweni. Lapho amacysts ekhula ebuchosheni, lesi simo sibizwa ngokuthi ineurocysticercosis. Izimpawu: ITaeniasis ivamise ukubonakala ngezimpawu ezithambile nezingaqondile ezihambisana nokutheleleka kwamathumbu (ubuhlungu besisu, isicanucanu, isifo sohudo, njll.). Lezi zimpawu zingaqhubeka kuze kube yilapho isikelemu siyafa ngemva kokwelashwa, ngaphandle kwalokho singaphila iminyaka.
translations.reference: While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.hypothesis: Although tapeworm eggs can infect humans, eggs from *T. solium* are typically only transmitted to people when they are ingested (through the fecal-oral route, or by consuming contaminated food or water). Infection with tapeworm parasites can cause a condition called cysticercosis, which affects various organs in the body, including the brain, muscles, and eyes. When the cysts develop in the brain, this condition is known as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis often presents with mild to moderate symptoms that are related to the intestinal infection (such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and nausea). These symptoms may resolve on their own after treatment, or they may persist for years.

translations.source: Ngecysticercosis, isikhathi sokufukamela siyahlukahluka, futhi abantu abangenwe yileli gciwane bangase bahlale bengabonakali iminyaka. Kwezinye izifunda ezitholakala kuphela, abantu abanaleli gciwane bangase babe namaqhuqhuva abonakalayo noma aphathekayo (iqhubu elincane eliqinile noma inode eqinile engabonwa ngokuthintwa) ngaphansi kwesikhumba. INeurocysticercosis ihlotshaniswa nezinhlobonhlobo zezimpawu nezimpawu kuye ngenombolo, usayizi, isigaba kanye nendawo yezinguquko ze-pathological kanye nokusabela kokuzivikela komzimba obambe iqhaza kanye ne-genotype ye-parasite, kodwa futhi ingaba i-asymptomatic emtholampilo. Izimpawu zingase zihlanganisa ubuhlungu bekhanda obungapheli, ukungaboni, ukuquleka (isithuthwane uma siphindaphinda), ihydrocephalus, imeningitis, ukuwohloka komqondo kanye nezimpawu ezibangelwa izilonda ezithatha izikhala zesimiso sezinzwa esimaphakathi. Ukwelashwa: Ukwelashwa kwetaeniasis ngeTaenia solium kubalulekile ukuvimbela ineurocysticercosis futhi njengethuluzi lokusiza ekulawuleni noma ekumiseni umjikelezo wokudluliselwa kwezinambuzane.
translations.reference: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle.
translations.hypothesis: Cysticercosis has a variable incubation period, and people infected with this parasite may remain asymptomatic for years. In some cases, individuals may develop visible or palpable nodules (small, firm lumps) under the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a wide range of symptoms and signs, depending on the number, size, location, and type of cysts, as well as the host's immune response and the parasite's genotype, but it can also be asymptomatic. Symptoms may include headache, seizures, vision problems, hydrocephalus, meningitis, cognitive impairment, and other neurological signs. Treatment: Treatment for cysticercosis caused by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and to help manage or eliminate the parasite.

translations.source: Ukwelashwa kungenziwa ezisekelweni zomuntu ngamunye, noma njengokuphathwa kwezidakamizwa ezinkulu kuye ngezimo zendawo kanye nezindlela zokulawula ezisetshenziswayo. ITaeniasis ingelashwa kusetshenziswa anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Kuneurocysticercosis, njengoba ukubhujiswa kwamacysts kungase kuholele ekuphenduleni kokuvuvukala, ukwelashwa okukhethekile kwesifo esisebenzayo kuyadingeka futhi kungase kuhlanganise izifundo ezinde ezinemithamo ephezulu ye-anthelmintic, kanye nokwesekwa kokwelashwa ngecorticosteroids kanye/noma izidakamizwa zokulwa nesithuthwane, futhi ngokunokwenzeka ukuhlinzwa. Umthamo kanye nobude besikhathi sokwelashwa kungahluka kakhulu futhi kuncike ikakhulukazi enanini, ubukhulu, indawo kanye nesigaba sokukhula samacysts, i-edema yawo yokuvuvukala ezungezile, ukushuba kanye nokuqina kwezimpawu zomtholampilo noma izimpawu.
translations.reference: The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.hypothesis: Treatment can be individualized based on the patient's specific circumstances, or it can involve the use of large-scale public health programs tailored to local conditions and control methods. Taeniasis is treated with anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). For neurocysticercosis, treatment may involve specific anti-parasitic medications, as well as corticosteroids or anti-inflammatory drugs, and may also include surgical intervention. The dosage and duration of treatment can vary widely and depend on factors such as the number, size, location, and stage of the cysts, the severity of the surrounding edema, the presence of complications, and the patient's overall health.

translations.source: I-Tetanus Uhlolojikelele: Itetanus yisifo esiyingozi esitholwa ngokuchayeka ezinhlamvu zebacterium, iClostridium tetani, ezihlala emhlabathini, amathe, uthuli kanye nomquba. Amagciwane angangena emzimbeni ngokusikeka okujulile, amanxeba noma ukusha okuthinta isimiso sezinzwa. Ukutheleleka kuholela ekufinyezweni kwemisipha okubuhlungu, ikakhulukazi emhlathini nasentanyeni, futhi ngokuvamile kwaziwa ngokuthi "ilockjaw". Abantu bayo yonke iminyaka bangathola itetanus kodwa lesi sifo sivame kakhulu futhi siyingozi ezinganeni ezisanda kuzalwa kanye nomama bazo lapho umama engavikelekile kutetanus ngomgomo, itetanus toxoid.
translations.reference: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid.
translations.hypothesis: Tetanus: Tetanus is a serious disease caused by bacteria, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, dust, and animal feces. The bacteria can enter the body through cuts, wounds, or puncture injuries. Tetanus causes painful muscle contractions, especially in the jaw and neck, and is often referred to as "lockjaw". People of all ages can get tetanus, but it is most common in infants and young children, as well as in adults who have not been vaccinated.

translations.source: Itetanus eyenzeka ngesikhathi sokukhulelwa noma emasontweni ayisi-6 ekupheleni kokukhulelwa ibizwa ngokuthi yi-tetanus kamama, kanti itetanus eyenzeka ezinsukwini zokuqala ezingu-28 zokuphila ibizwa ngokuthi itetanus yengane. Lesi sifo sihlala siyinkinga yezempilo yomphakathi ebalulekile ezingxenyeni eziningi zomhlaba, kodwa ikakhulukazi emazweni anemali ephansi noma ezifundeni, lapho ukugonywa kuphansi futhi izindlela zokuzalwa ezingcolile zivamile. IWHO ilinganisela ukuthi ngo-2018 (unyaka wakamuva lapho izilinganiso zitholakala), izingane ezisanda kuzalwa ezingu-25 000 zabulawa yitetanus esanda kuzalwa, ukwehla okungama-88% esimweni sango-2000. Izimpawu: Cishe zonke izehlakalo zetetanus zenzeka kubantu abangakaze bagonywe noma kubantu abadala abangazange bahlale benolwazi lwakamuva ngezithombe zabo ezikhuthazayo. Izimpawu nezimpawu zetetanus zivela noma nini kusukela ezinsukwini ezi-3 kuya kwezingu-21 ngemva kokuba amagciwane etetanus engene emzimbeni ngesilonda.
translations.reference: Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.hypothesis: Tetanus that occurs during pregnancy or in the first 6 weeks after delivery is known as maternal tetanus, while tetanus that occurs in the first 28 days of life is known as neonatal tetanus. This disease remains a significant public health problem in many parts of the world, particularly in low-income countries and developing countries, where access to vaccination and healthcare is limited. The WHO estimates that in 2018 (the most recent year for which data are available), 25,000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, a decrease of 88% since 2000. Symptoms: The symptoms of tetanus can occur anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.

translations.source: Izigameko eziningi zenzeka phakathi nezinsuku eziyi-14. Izimpawu zingabandakanya: umhlathi noma ukungakwazi ukuvula umlomo ama-muscle spasms ngokuvamile ngemuva, isisu kanye namaphethelo imisipha ebuhlungu engazelelwe ngokuvamile ebangelwa imisindo engazelelwe
translations.reference: Most cases occur within 14 days. Symptoms can include: jaw cramping or the inability to open the mouth muscle spasms often in the back, abdomen and extremities sudden painful muscle spasms often triggered by sudden noises
translations.hypothesis: Many symptoms occur over a period of 14 days. These may include: difficulty swallowing or being unable to open the mouth, muscle spasms, pain in the jaw and throat, and hoarseness.

translations.source: inkinga yokugwinya ukuquleka ukuphathwa ikhanda umkhuhlane nokujuluka izinguquko kumfutho wegazi noma ukushaya kwenhliziyo okusheshayo.
translations.reference: trouble swallowing seizures headache fever and sweating changes in blood pressure or fast heart rate.
translations.hypothesis: treatment for shock, including managing blood pressure and heart rate changes, as well as addressing underlying causes such as blood clots or rapid heart rate.

translations.source: Kutetanus yosana olusanda kuzalwa, izimpawu zifaka ukugedla kwemisipha, okuvame ukwandulelwa ukungakwazi komntwana ukuncela noma ukuncelisa, kanye nokukhala ngokweqile. Itetanus itholakala ngesisekelo sezici zomtholampilo futhi ayidingi ukuqinisekiswa kwelabhorethri. Itetanus idinga ukwelashwa endaweni yezokwelapha, ngokuvamile esibhedlela sokudlulisela. Abantu abelulama ku-tetanus abanawo amandla okuzivikela emvelo futhi bangatheleleka futhi ngakho ke badinga ukugonywa. Ukuvimbela: Itetanus ingavinjelwa ngokugonywa ngemithi yokugoma equkethe itetanus-toxoid-containing vaccines (TTCV), efakwe ezinhlelweni zokugoma ezijwayelekile emhlabeni jikelele futhi inikezwe ngesikhathi sokuxhumana nokunakekelwa kwabakhulelwe.
translations.reference: In neonatal tetanus, symptoms include muscle spasms, which are often preceded by the newborn’s inability to suck or breastfeed, and excessive crying. Tetanus is diagnosed on the basis of clinical features and does not require laboratory confirmation. Tetanus requires treatment in a medical facility, often in a referral hospital. People who recover from tetanus do not have natural immunity and can be infected again and therefore need to be immunized. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented through immunization with tetanus-toxoid-containing vaccines (TTCV), which are included in routine immunization programmes globally and administered during antenatal care contacts.
translations.hypothesis: Tetanus is a serious bacterial infection that affects the nervous system. Symptoms include muscle stiffness, spasms, and difficulty swallowing. Tetanus is caused by a bacterium called Clostridium tetani, which is found in soil and animal feces. The bacteria enters the body through a wound. Tetanus is a serious illness that can be fatal. It is treated with antibiotics and supportive care. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented by vaccination. The tetanus vaccine is given in a series of shots, usually starting in infancy.

translations.source: Ukuze uvikelwe kukho konke ukuphila, iWHO incoma ukuthi umuntu ngamunye athole imithamo engu-6 (imithamo emi-3 eyinhloko kanye nemi-3 ye-booster) yeTTCV. Uchungechunge oluyinhloko lwemithamo emi-3 kufanele luqale ekuqaleni kwamasonto ayi-6 ubudala, imithamo elandelayo inikezwe ngesikhawu esincane samaviki ama-4 phakathi kwemithamo. Imithamo emi-3 ye-booster kufanele inikezwe phakathi nonyaka wesibili wokuphila (izinyanga eziyi-12-23), ineminyaka engu-4-7 ubudala, kanye neminyaka engu-9-15 ubudala. Ngokufanelekile, kufanele okungenani kube neminyaka emi-4 phakathi kwemithamo ye-booster. Kunezinhlobo eziningi zemithi yokugoma esetshenziselwa ukuvikela itetanus: imigomo yediphtheria kanye netetanus (DT) idiphtheria, itetanus, nepertussis (ukukhwehlela) (DTaP) vaccinestetanus kanye ne-diphtheria (Td) vaccinestetanus, diphtheria, ne-pertussis (Tdap) imigomo.
translations.reference: To be protected throughout life, WHO recommends that an individual receives 6 doses (3 primary plus 3 booster doses) of TTCV. The 3-dose primary series should begin as early as 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given with a minimum interval of 4 weeks between doses. The 3 booster doses should preferably be given during the second year of life (12–23 months), at 4–7 years of age, and at 9–15 years of age. Ideally, there should be at least 4 years between booster doses. There are many kinds of vaccines used to protect against tetanus: diphtheria and tetanus (DT) vaccinesdiphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (whooping cough) (DTaP) vaccinestetanus and diphtheria (Td) vaccinestetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (Tdap) vaccines.
translations.hypothesis: In order to provide complete protection, the WHO recommends that each person receive six doses of the tetanus toxoid (TT vaccine): three primary doses and three booster doses. The primary series of three doses should begin at the start of the sixth month of life, with each dose administered four weeks apart. The three booster doses should be administered between the second and third years of life (between 12 and 36 months), at 4-7 years of age, and at 9-15 years of age. Ideally, there should be at least four years between booster doses. There are several different types of tetanus vaccines available, including: diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (DTP) vaccines; diphtheria, tetanus, and acellular pertussis (DTaP) vaccines; and diphtheria, tetanus, and acellular pertussis (Tdap) vaccines.

translations.source: Itetanus esanda kuzalwa ingavinjelwa ngokugoma abesifazane abaseminyakeni yokuzala ngeTTCV, kungaba ngesikhathi sokukhulelwa noma ngaphandle kokukhulelwa. Ukwengeza, izinqubo zezokwelapha eziqinile zingase futhi zivikele isifo setetanus okuhlanganisa ukulethwa okuhlanzekile nokunakekelwa kwentambo ngesikhathi sokubeletha, nokunakekelwa okufanele kwenxeba ngezinqubo zokuhlinzwa nezinyo.
translations.reference: Neonatal tetanus can be prevented by immunizing women of reproductive age with TTCV, either during pregnancy or outside of pregnancy. Additionally, robust medical practices can also prevent tetanus disease including clean delivery and cord care during childbirth, and proper wound care for surgical and dental procedures.
translations.hypothesis: Newborn babies can be protected from tetanus by giving them a tetanus immunoglobulin injection, either at birth or shortly thereafter. In addition, certain medical procedures, such as a clean delivery and proper cord care, can also help to prevent tetanus.

translations.source: I-encephalitis ephethwe umkhaza (Tick-borne encephalitis) Uhlolojikelele: I-encephalitis ethwala umkhaza okuyiTick-borne encephalitis (TBE) iyimbangela ebalulekile yokutheleleka ngegciwane ohlelweni lwezinzwa olumaphakathi emazweni asempumalanga, enkabeni, asenyakatho kanye nasanda ngokwandayo entshonalanga ye-Europe, nasenyakatho yeChina, iMongolia, kanye neRussian Federation. Igciwane le-encephalitis elithwala umkhaza liyilungu lomndeni wakwaFlaviviridae. Cishe izigameko ezingu-10 000–12 000 zomtholampilo ze-encephalitis ezithwalwa umkhaza zibikwa unyaka ngamunye, kodwa lesi sibalo kukholakala ukuthi siphansi kakhulu kunengqikithi yenani langempela lamacala omtholampilo. Izifo eziningi ezinaleli gciwane zibangelwa imikhaza ethelelekile, evame ukuhlala iqinile esikhumbeni izinsuku eziningi.
translations.reference: Tick-borne encephalitis Overview: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is an important cause of viral infections of the central nervous system in eastern, central, northern and increasingly western European countries, and in northern China, Mongolia, and the Russian Federation. Tick-borne encephalitis virus is a member of the family Flaviviridae. Approximately 10 000–12 000 clinical cases of tick-borne encephalitis are reported each year, but this figure is believed to be significantly lower than the actual total number of clinical cases. The vast majority of infections with the virus result from infected ticks, which often remain firmly attached to the skin for days.
translations.hypothesis: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE): Overview: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is a significant public health concern in the temperate regions of Europe, Asia, and North America. The disease is caused by a virus belonging to the Flaviviridae family. Each year, an estimated 10,000–12,000 cases of TBE are reported, but this number is likely an underestimate of the true incidence of the disease. The virus is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected ticks, which typically feed on animals for several days.

translations.source: Ezikhathini ezingavamile, ukutheleleka kungavela ngokuphuza ubisi olungagcotshwanga oluvela ezimbuzini, izimvu noma izinkomo. Abantu bahlangana nemikhaza ngesikhathi semisebenzi yangaphandle ezindaweni ezinamahlathi afinyelela ukuphakama okungamamitha angama-2000. Akukho ukudluliswa okuqondile komuntu kuya kumuntu. Izimpawu: Ukutheleleka okuningi kuhlala kungabonakali. Esimeni sokugula okusemtholampilo, isikhathi sokufukamela se-encephalitis ethwalwa umkhaza sithatha izinsuku ezingu-2-28 (imvamisa izinsuku ezingu-7-14) futhi silandelwa izinsuku ezingu-1-8 zezimpawu ezijwayelekile zomkhuhlane, njengokukhathala, ikhanda elibuhlungu kanye nokugula okuvamile, ngokuvamile. kuhlanganiswe nemfiva engu-≥38 °C.
translations.reference: On rare occasions, infection can result from consumption of unpasteurized milk from infect goats, sheep or cows. People come in contact with the ticks during outdoor activities in forested areas up to an altitude of about 2000 meters. There is no direct person-to-person transmission. Symptoms: Most infections remain asymptomatic. In case of clinical illness, the incubation period for tick-borne encephalitis lasts 2–28 days (most commonly 7–14 days) and is followed by 1–8 days of general cold symptoms, such as fatigue, headache and general malaise, usually combined with fever of ≥38 °C.
translations.hypothesis: In rare cases, encephalitis can be caused by a non-A type virus, originating from birds, pigs, or cattle. People may come into contact with these viruses during outdoor activities in areas with high bird, pig, or cattle populations. There is no direct transmission from person to person. Symptoms: Encephalitis is often not immediately apparent. In cases of encephalitis caused by a virus, the incubation period can range from 2-28 days (typically 7-14 days), followed by a period of 1-8 days of typical flu-like symptoms, such as fever, headache, and body aches, often accompanied by a temperature of ≥38 °C.

translations.source: Ngemuva kokuphumula okungabonakali kwezinsuku ezingu-1-20 kufika ku-15% weziguli ziba nesigaba sesibili sesifo esibonakala ngemfiva evamise ukudlula ku-40 °C kanye nezimpawu zokuthinteka kwesimiso sezinzwa esimaphakathi, njenge-meningitis (isb. imfiva, ikhanda elibuhlungu, nokuqina. intamo), i-encephalitis (isb, ukozela, ukudideka, nokuphazamiseka kwezinzwa), imyelitis noma iradiculitis. Ukukhula kwe-encephalitis phakathi nalesi sigaba sesibili kungase kubangele ukukhubazeka, isequelae unomphela noma ukufa. Cishe i-1% yamacala ane-neurological pathologies angafa; amazinga okufa aphezulu abikiwe evela kumfelandawonye waseRussia, okungenzeka ahlotshaniswe nohlobo oluncane lwegciwane oluhlukile. Ubunzima bokugula buyanda ngokuya ngeminyaka yesiguli, kodwa ukufa kuye kwabikwa kuzo zonke izinhlobo zeminyaka. Akukho ukwelashwa okuqondile kwe-encephalitis ephethwe umkhaza.
translations.reference: After an asymptomatic interval of 1–20 days up to 15% of patients experience a second phase of the disease characterized by fever frequently exceeding 40 °C and signs of central nervous system involvement, such as meningitis (e.g. fever, headache, and a stiff neck), encephalitis (e.g., drowsiness, confusion, and sensory disturbances), myelitis or radiculitis. Encephalitis developing during this second phase may result in paralysis, permanent sequelae or death. About 1% of cases with neurological pathologies may die; higher fatality rates have been reported from the Russian federation, which may be related to a different virus subtype. Severity of illness increases with age of the patient, but fatalities have been reported from all age groups. There is no specific treatment for tick-borne encephalitis.
translations.hypothesis: Following a period of apparent rest of 1-20 days, 15% of patients developed a second stage of the illness, characterized by a fever of over 40°C and neurological symptoms such as meningitis (e.g., headache, stiff neck, and sensitivity to light), encephalitis (e.g., confusion, seizures, and sensory disturbances), or myelitis/radiculitis. The development of encephalitis in this second stage could lead to complications, long-term sequelae, or death. Approximately 1% of patients with neurological pathologies died; higher mortality rates have been reported from Russia, possibly due to a specific strain of the virus. The severity of the illness increased with age, but mortality was observed in all age groups. There is no specific treatment for encephalitis caused by this virus.

translations.source: Ukuvimbela: Abantu bangazivikela emikhazani ngokugqoka izingubo ezifanele, okuhlanganisa ibhulukwe elide nezicathulo ezivaliwe, lapho beqwala izintaba noma bekanise emazweni noma ezindaweni ezisengozini. Umzimba wonke kufanele uhlolwe nsuku zonke futhi imikhaza enamathiselwe ikhishwe ngokushesha ngangokunokwenzeka. Ukusetshenziswa kwemikhiqizo yobisi engasetshenziswanga kufanele futhi kugwenywe kulezo zindawo. Ukugoma kunikeza isivikelo esisebenza kahle kakhulu. Njengamanje, kunemithi yokugoma emi-4 esetshenziswa kabanzi yekhwalithi eqinisekisiwe:
translations.reference: Prevention: People can protect themselves from ticks by wearing appropriate clothing, including long trousers and closed footwear, when hiking or camping in countries or areas at risk. The whole body should be inspected daily and attached ticks removed as soon as possible. The consumption of unpasteurized dairy products should also be avoided in those areas. Immunization offers the most effective protection. Currently, there are 4 widely used vaccines of assured quality:
translations.hypothesis: Prevention: People can protect themselves from falls by wearing appropriate clothing, including long sleeves and sturdy shoes, when working on slopes or in uneven terrain. All individuals should be checked daily, and any injuries should be treated immediately. The use of non-slip surfaces should also be considered in these areas. Fall protection equipment is also available, including harnesses and ropes. There are currently four types of fall protection equipment that are widely used and considered to be of high quality:

translations.source: iFSME-Immun ne-Encepur, ekhiqizwa e-Austria naseGermany ngokulandelana, futhi isekelwe ezinhlotsheni zegciwane zase-Europe; futhi iTBE-Moscow ne-EnceVir, ekhiqizwa e-Russian Federation futhi isekelwe ezinhlobonhlobo ze-Far-Eastern. Imithi yokugoma emi-4 ithathwa njengephephile futhi isebenza ngempumelelo. Ezindaweni lapho lesi sifo sigcwele khona kakhulu, iWHO itusa ukuba kugonywe kuzo zonke izinhlobo zeminyaka, kuhlanganise nezingane. Imikhaza iphinde idlulisele iBorreliosis (iLyme disease), okuyisifo esibangelwa amagciwane.
translations.reference: FSME-Immun and Encepur, manufactured in Austria and Germany respectively, and based on European strains of the virus; and TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, manufactured in the Russian Federation and based on Far-Eastern strains. The 4 vaccines are considered to be safe and effective. In areas where the disease is highly endemic, WHO recommends that vaccination be offered to all age groups, including children. Ticks also transmit Borreliosis (Lyme disease), which is a bacterial infection.
translations.hypothesis: FSME-Immun and Encepur, manufactured in Austria and Germany respectively, and based on European tick-borne disease vaccines; and TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, manufactured in the Russian Federation and based on Far-Eastern tick-borne disease vaccines. The four tick-borne diseases are considered serious and can cause significant health problems. In areas where these diseases are prevalent, the WHO recommends that all individuals, including children, be vaccinated. Tick-borne diseases can also transmit Borreliosis (Lyme disease), a bacterial infection.

translations.source: Ukugonywa kweTBE akusebenzi ukulwa nalesi sifo, kodwa esilapheka ngama-antimicrobial.
translations.reference: TBE vaccination is not effective against this disease, which however is treatable with antimicrobials.
translations.hypothesis: While TBE vaccination is not a cure for the disease, it does provide protection against it.

translations.source: Ugwayi Uhlolojikelele: iNicotine equkethwe kugwayi iyalutha kakhulu futhi ukusetshenziswa kukagwayi kuyingozi enkulu yezifo zenhliziyo nemithambo yegazi, izinhlobo ezingaphezu kwama-20 ezihlukene noma izinhlobo ezincane zomdlavuza, nezinye izimo eziningi zempilo eziwohlozayo. Unyaka ngamunye, abantu abangaphezu kwezigidi ezingu-8 bayafa ngenxa yokubhema ugwayi. Ukufa okuningi okuhlobene nogwayi kwenzeka emazweni anemali ephansi nephakathi, ngokuvamile okuyizisulu zokugxambukela kwemboni kagwayi nokumaketha. Ugwayi ungaba yingozi nakubantu abangabhemi.
translations.reference: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers.
translations.hypothesis: General Overview: Nicotine, the active ingredient in tobacco, is highly addictive and its use poses a significant risk of heart disease, stroke, and various types of cancer. Each year, more than 8 million people die worldwide as a result of tobacco use. The majority of these deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, often due to poverty and limited access to healthcare. Tobacco use also poses a risk to non-smokers through secondhand smoke.

translations.source: Ukuchayeka kwentuthu kagwayi kuye kwaba nesandla emiphumeleni emibi yezempilo, okubangela ukufa kwabantu abayizigidi eziyi-1.2 ngonyaka. Cishe ingxenye yazo zonke izingane ziphefumula umoya ongcoliswe intuthu kagwayi kanti izingane ezingu-65 000 ziyafa unyaka ngamunye ngenxa yezifo ezihlobene nentuthu kagwayi. Ukubhema ngenkathi ukhulelwe kungaholela ezimweni eziningana zezempilo zezingane. Imikhiqizo kagwayi eshisayo (amaHTP) iqukethe ugwayi futhi ichaya abasebenzisi ekukhiqizweni okunobuthi, iningi labo elidala umdlavuza futhi eliyingozi empilweni. Izinhlelo ze-electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) kanye ne-electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), ngokuvamile ezaziwa ngokuthi i-e-cigarette, azinawo ugwayi futhi zingase zibe nenicotine noma zingaqukathi, kodwa ziyingozi empilweni futhi ngokungangabazeki aziphephile.
translations.reference: Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe.
translations.hypothesis: Exposure to secondhand smoke from cigarettes is a major public health problem, contributing to an estimated 1.2 million deaths each year. Approximately half of all children are exposed to secondhand smoke, and an estimated 65,000 children die each year as a result of illnesses related to secondhand smoke. Exposure to secondhand smoke during childhood can lead to a variety of health problems. Cigarette smoke contains numerous harmful chemicals, including nicotine, which can have a negative impact on children's health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain nicotine, but they can still be harmful to children's health. Electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), also known as e-cigarettes, do not contain nicotine, but they can still be harmful to children's health.

translations.source: Nokho, kusesekuseni kakhulu ukunikeza impendulo ecacile kumthelela wesikhathi eside wamaHTP kanye/noma ukusetshenziswa kwe-e-cigarette. Umthelela: Kulinganiselwa ukuthi abantu abayizigidi eziyizinkulungwane ezingu-1.3 emhlabeni wonke basebenzisa ugwayi, u-80% wabo usemazweni anemali ephansi naphakathi. Ukusetshenziswa kukagwayi kunomthelela ebuphofini ngokususa imali yasendlini isuke ezidingweni eziyisisekelo, njengokudla nendawo yokuhlala, iye kugwayi. Lokhu kuziphatha kokuchitha kunzima ukunqanda ngoba ugwayi uyalutha kakhulu. Kuphinde kubangele ukufa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kanye nokukhubazeka kwabantu abadala abaneminyaka ekhiqizayo emakhaya ngaleyo ndlela kuholele ekwehleni kwemali engenayo yasekhaya kanye nezindleko zokunakekelwa kwezempilo eziphakeme.
translations.reference: However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs.
translations.hypothesis: However, the primary concern is the widespread use of heroin and/or e-cigarettes. Statistics indicate that over 1.3 million people worldwide are addicted to heroin, with 80% of them in low- and middle-income countries. The use of heroin has a devastating impact on individuals and families, leading to the loss of income, homelessness, and increased healthcare costs.

translations.source: Ngaphezu komthelela omubi kagwayi kwezempilo, isamba sezindleko zomnotho zokubhema (kusukela ezindlekweni zezempilo kanye nokulahlekelwa umkhiqizo ndawonye) zilinganiselwa ku-US$1.4 trillion ngonyaka, okulingana no-1.8% wesamba semali sasekhaya sonyaka emhlabeni somkhiqizo (GDP). Cishe u-40% walezi zindleko wenzeka emazweni asathuthuka, okugqamisa umthwalo onzima la mazwe awuthwele. Izintela zikagwayi zisetshenziswa ngokwengxenye ukulwa nalo mphumela futhi zibonwa njengendlela engabizi kakhulu yokunqanda ukusetshenziswa kukagwayi, ikakhulukazi phakathi kwentsha kanye nabantu abahola kancane. Ukwenyuka kwentela okwandisa amanani entengo kagwayi ngo-10% kunciphisa ukusetshenziswa kukagwayi cishe ngo-4% emazweni anemali engenayo ephezulu futhi cishe ngo-5% emazweni anemali engenayo esezingeni eliphansi noma ezingeni eliphakathi. Impendulo yeWHO: Izinga lale nhlekelele yabantu nezomnotho liyashaqisa, kodwa futhi liyagwemeka.
translations.reference: In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable.
translations.hypothesis: Despite the negative impact on public health, the economic costs of air pollution are estimated to be around US$1.4 trillion per year, which is about 1.8% of global GDP. Roughly 40% of these costs are borne by developing countries, placing a significant burden on these nations. Taxes on fossil fuels are used in part to offset these costs, and are seen as a key way to discourage the use of fossil fuels, particularly among young people and those with lower incomes. An increase in fuel taxes of 10% can reduce fossil fuel consumption by about 4% in countries with high incomes, and by about 5% in countries with low or middle incomes.

translations.source: Ugwayi Omkhulu - kanye nabo bonke abakhiqizi bemikhiqizo kagwayi - balwela ukuqinisekisa ukuthi izingozi zemikhiqizo yabo ziyafihlwa, kodwa siyazilwela: Ngo-2003, Amazwe Angamalungu eWHO amukela ngazwi linye IWHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), i isivumelwano sezempilo yomphakathi kuphela ngaphansi kweWHO. Isebenza kusukela ngo-2005, njengamanje inamaqembu ayi-181. Ukusiza amazwe ukuthi asebenzise i-WHO FCTC, iWHO yethule iMPOWER, iphakethe lezinyathelo zobuchwepheshe nezinsiza, ngayinye ehambelana nokunikezwa okungenani okukodwa kweWHO FCTC. iMPOWER yakha amandla amazwe okusebenzisa izinyathelo eziyisi-6 zokunciphisa isidingo semikhiqizo kagwayi: ukuqapha ukusetshenziswa kukagwayi nezinqubomgomo zokuvimbela, ukuvikela abantu ekusetshenzisweni kukagwayi, ukunikeza usizo lokuyeka ugwayi, ukuxwayisa ngezingozi zikagwayi, ukuphoqelela ukuvinjelwa kokukhangisa kukagwayi, ukuphromotha nokuxhaswa kanye nokukhuphula izintela kugwayi.
translations.reference: Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization (WHO), along with all tobacco product manufacturers, have been working to ensure that the dangers of tobacco products are properly disclosed. However, they have faced challenges in this effort. In 2003, the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC) was adopted, a global treaty aimed at protecting public health through tobacco control. It has been in effect since 2005, and currently has 181 member states. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, the WHO has developed MPOWER, a set of evidence-based tools and resources that are designed to support the implementation of the WHO FCTC's six key measures: monitoring tobacco use and exposure, promoting smokefree policies, implementing tobacco control legislations, providing smokefree offices and public spaces, ensuring tobacco product regulation, and implementing tobacco control media campaigns.

translations.source: ITrachoma Uhlolojikelele: ITrachoma yisifo seso futhi iyimbangela ehamba phambili ethathelanayo yobumpumputhe emhlabeni wonke. Ibangelwa i-obligate intracellular bacterium ebizwa ngokuthi iChlamydia trachomatis. Ukutheleleka kudluliselwa kusuka kumuntu kuya komunye ngokudluliswa okuqondile noma okungaqondile kokuphuma kwamehlo nasemakhaleni abantu abanaleli gciwane; ukudluliswa okungaqondile kubandakanya inqola emzimbeni wezinhlobo zezimpukane. Izingane ezifunda enkulisa ziphethe i-reservoir eyinhloko yokutheleleka.
translations.reference: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection.
translations.hypothesis: ITrachoma Overview: Trachoma is a contagious eye infection and the leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide. It is caused by the obligate intracellular bacterium, Chlamydia trachomatis. Transmission occurs from person to person through direct or indirect contact with infected eyes or bodily fluids; indirect transmission can also occur through contaminated surfaces. Children are particularly vulnerable to infection.

translations.source: Amamodeli aphakamisa ukuthi umuntu udinga izifo ezingaphezu kuka-150 zokuphila kwakhe konke ukuze athuthukise izinkinga zokungaboni ze-trachoma. ITrachoma igcwele abantu abampofu kakhulu emhlabeni, abahlala emaphandleni nasezindaweni ezikude futhi abangenawo ngokwanele amanzi, ukuthuthwa kwendle kanye nokunakekelwa kwezempilo. I-Afrika iyizwekazi elithinteke kakhulu, kodwa futhi inemizamo yokulawula esabalele kakhulu. Lesi sifo sitholakala naseCentral naseNingizimu Melika, e-Asia, e-Australia naseMiddle East. Kusukela ngomhla ka-2 kuMasingana 2020, amazwe ayi-13 abike ukuthi afinyelele izinhloso zokuqeda.
translations.reference: Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals.
translations.hypothesis: Studies suggest that a person needs more than 150 different types of food to fully develop and maintain their vision, highlighting the importance of a balanced and nutritious diet for preventing trachoma. Trachoma is a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide, affecting primarily children in poor, rural communities with limited access to clean water, sanitation, and healthcare. Africa is particularly affected, but the disease is also prevalent in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. Since September 2020, 13 countries have pledged to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.

translations.source: Ngo-2019 kuphela, abantu abangama-92 622 bathola ukwelashwa kokuhlinzwa kwetrachoma esezingeni eliphakeme futhi abayizigidi ezingama-95.2 balashwe ngama-antibiotics. Naphezu kwalezi mpumelelo, lesi sifo siseyinkinga enkulu yezempilo yomphakathi, ngokulahleka okulinganiselwa ku-US$ 8 billion ngonyaka ekukhiqizeni ngenxa yokungaboni nokungaboni kahle. Izimpawu: Ukutheleleka kuhlotshaniswa nezinguquko zokuvuvukala kuconjunctiva eyaziwa ngokuthi "itrachoma esebenzayo". Amasosha omzimba womuntu angasusa isiqephu esisodwa sokutheleleka, kodwa emiphakathini ehlala njalo, ukutholwa kabusha kwento ephilayo kwenzeka kaningi. Ngemva kweminyaka yeziqephu eziphindaphindiwe zetrachoma esebenzayo, ingaphakathi lejwabu leso lingase libe nezibazi ezimbi kakhulu (itrachomatous conjunctival scarring) kangangokuthi iphendukela ngaphakathi futhi ibangele ukuthi izinkophe zihlikihle esweni (trachomatous trichiasis), okuholela ebuhlungu obuqhubekayo nokungabekezelelani okuncane; lokhu kanye nezinye izinguquko zeso kungaholela emazingeni ecornea.
translations.reference: In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea.
translations.hypothesis: In 2019, 92,622 people received treatment for trachoma, a severe eye infection, and over 95.2 million people were exposed to it. Despite these efforts, trachoma remains a major public health problem, costing an estimated $8 billion annually in lost productivity due to disability and blindness. Symptoms: Trachoma is characterized by inflammation and changes in the conjunctiva, the clear membrane that covers the white part of the eye. While a single episode of trachoma can be treated, the infection often recurs. After repeated episodes of trachoma, the inner surface of the eyelids can become scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring), causing the eyelashes to turn inward and rub against the eye (trachomatous trichiasis). This can lead to chronic pain, reduced vision, and even blindness; in addition, other complications of trachoma can affect the cornea.

translations.source: Uma singelashwa, lesi simo siholela ekwakhekeni kwe-opacities engalungiseki, okuholela ekukhubazekeni kokubona noma ubumpumputhe. Iminyaka yobudala okwenzeka ngayo lokhu incike ezintweni ezimbalwa ezihlanganisa namandla okudlulisela kwasendaweni. Emiphakathini evame kakhulu, kungenzeka ebuntwaneni, nakuba ukuqala kokukhubazeka kokubona phakathi kweminyaka yobudala engama-30 kuya kwengama-40 kuvame kakhulu. Abesifazane baphuphuthekiswa izikhathi ezine ngokuphindwe kane kunabesilisa, mhlawumbe ngenxa yokuxhumana njalo nezingane ezinegciwane. Abantu abahlala emiphakathini empofu kakhulu nekude kakhulu yase-Afrika, e-Asia, e-Australia, eMiddle East, eMelika Ephakathi neseNingizimu kanye naseziqhingini zasePacific basengozini.
translations.reference: Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk.
translations.hypothesis: When we are exposed to these conditions, it can lead to the development of cataracts, which is a clouding of the lens of the eye, resulting in blurred or distorted vision. The age at which this typically occurs varies depending on several factors, including genetics and environmental exposures. In general, cataracts are more common in people over the age of 40, although they can develop earlier in some cases. Women are also more likely to develop cataracts than men, possibly due to hormonal changes associated with menopause. People who live in areas with high levels of sunlight exposure, such as those in Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, the Americas, and the Pacific Islands, are at higher risk.

translations.source: Ukwelashwa: ITrachoma ingavinjelwa ngokunciphisa izici eziyingozi zemvelo. Lokhu kubandakanya ukungahlanzeki ngokwanele, amakhaya aminyene, ukungakwazi ukuthola amanzi ngokwanele kanye nokungatholakali okwanele nokusetshenziswa kwezindlu zangasese. Amagciwane ngokuvamile aqala esemncane futhi akhule kancane futhi abe mfushane ngokuhamba kwesikhathi ngokuya ngeminyaka. Ukutheleleka kuvame ukutholwa ngomndeni kanye nokuxhumana nabanye abanesifo esisebenzayo. Ukutheleleka okuphindaphindiwe kubangela izimpawu ezimbi kakhulu zesifo.
translations.reference: Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease.
translations.hypothesis: Treatment: Trachoma is treated by reducing the risk factors that contribute to the condition. This includes proper hygiene, avoiding close contact with infected individuals, ensuring adequate access to clean water, and avoiding the use of contaminated materials. The infection typically starts in early childhood and progresses slowly over time. It is often transmitted through close contact with infected individuals. Severe cases of trachoma can lead to serious complications.

translations.source: ITrachoma ingaqedwa njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi kusetshenziswa isu elithi SAFE, elihlanganisa: Ukuhlinzwa ukwelapha itrachomatous trichiasis, isigaba sokuphuphuthekisa lesi sifo; Ama-antibiotics ukususa ukutheleleka, ikakhulukazi ukuphathwa kwezidakamizwa eziningi ze-antibiotic azithromycin, enikelwa ngumkhiqizi ezinhlelweni zikazwelonke nge-International Trachoma Initiative; Ukuhlanzeka kobuso; futhi Ukuthuthukiswa kwemvelo, ikakhulukazi ukutholakala kwamanzi nokuthuthwa kwendle okungcono.
translations.reference: Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation.
translations.hypothesis: Trachoma can be addressed as a public health issue through a program called SAFE, which includes: Treatment for trachomatous trichiasis, including surgical procedures; Antibiotics to eliminate infection, particularly the use of azithromycin, which is provided by the International Trachoma Initiative in national programs; Facial hygiene; and Promotion of improved sanitation, particularly access to clean water and better hygiene practices.

translations.source: IWHO yamukela isu leSAFE ku-1993 futhi iyaqhubeka nomsebenzi wayo wokuhlinzeka ngobuholi kanye nokuxhumana kwemizamo yamazwe ngamazwe yokuqeda i-trachoma njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi.
translations.reference: WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues its work in providing global leadership and coordinating international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.

translations.source: Umuthi Wendabuko, Ohambisanayo kanye Nodidiyelo Uhlolojikelele: Imithi yesintu Imithi yesintu inomlando omude. Yisamba esiphelele solwazi, ikhono, nezinqubo ezisekelwe emibonweni, izinkolelo, kanye nolwazi lwendabuko lwamasiko ahlukene, kungakhathaliseki ukuthi luyachayeka noma cha, olusetshenziswa ekunakekeleni impilo kanye nasekuvimbeleni, ekuxilongeni, ekuthuthukiseni noma ekwelapheni. yokugula ngokomzimba nangokwengqondo. Umuthi ohambisanayo
translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine
translations.hypothesis: The Art of Herbal Medicine: Herbal medicine has a long and rich history. It is a holistic approach to health and well-being that uses plants and other natural substances to promote healing, prevent illness, and improve overall quality of life. Herbal medicine can be used for a wide range of conditions, both physical and mental.

translations.source: Amagama athi “imithi ehambisanayo” noma “umuthi ohlukile” asho iqoqo elibanzi lezinqubo zokunakekelwa kwezempilo ezingeyona ingxenye yesiko lalelo zwe noma imithi evamile futhi engahlanganisiwe ngokugcwele ohlelweni lokunakekelwa kwezempilo olubusayo. Asetshenziswa ngokushintshana nemithi yesintu kwamanye amazwe. Imithi yamakhambi Imithi yamakhambi ihlanganisa amakhambi ayizinhlobonhlobo, izinto zeherbal, amalungiselelo eherbal nemikhiqizo yeherbal eqediwe, equkethe izithako ezisebenzayo izingxenye zezitshalo, noma ezinye izinto zezitshalo, noma izinhlanganisela. Umthelela: Isu Lemithi Yendabuko yeWHO 2014–2023 lathuthukiswa futhi laqalwa ngokuphendula isinqumo seWorld Health Assembly semithi yendabuko (WHA62.13).
translations.reference: The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis: Terms such as "medicinal plant" or "herbal medicine" refer to a wide range of plant-based therapies that are not part of mainstream medicine or conventional herbal medicine, and are not fully integrated into standard healthcare systems. They are often used interchangeably with "traditional medicine" or "herbal medicine." Herbal remedies include a variety of preparations, such as herbal extracts, herbal formulations, and finished herbal products, which contain active ingredients derived from plants or other natural sources. The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to a resolution of the World Health Assembly on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).

translations.source: Isu lihlose ukusekela Amazwe Angamalungu ekuthuthukiseni izinqubomgomo ezisebenzayo kanye nokusebenzisa izinhlelo zokusebenza ezizoqinisa indima edlalwa imithi yendabuko ekugcineni imiphakathi iphilile. Ukubhekana nezinselele, ukusabela ezidingweni ezihlonzwe Amazwe Angamalungu kanye nokwakhela phezu komsebenzi owenziwe ngaphansi kwesu lemithi yendabuko yeWHO: 2002-2005, isu elibuyekeziwe lesikhathi sika-2014-2023 linikezela ukunaka okungaphezulu kunokwandulela walo ekubekeni phambili izinsiza zezempilo nezinhlelo. , okuhlanganisa imikhiqizo yemithi yesintu nehambisanayo, izinqubo kanye nodokotela. Izinjongo zamasu yilezi: Ukwakha isisekelo solwazi sokuphatha okusebenzayo kweT&CM ngokusebenzisa izinqubomgomo zikazwelonke ezifanele; Ukuqinisa ukuqinisekiswa kwekhwalithi, ukuphepha, ukusetshenziswa okufanele nokusebenza kahle kweT&CM ngokulawula imikhiqizo, izinqubo kanye nabasebenzi;
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners;
translations.hypothesis: The project aims to support member countries in developing and implementing effective policies and practices that strengthen the role of traditional medicine in preventing and managing public health problems. In response to challenges, and in line with the principles of the WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy and Action Plan: 2002-2005, this updated project for the period 2014-2023 provides enhanced support for the development of national policies and programs, including the production and distribution of herbal medicines, the training of traditional medicine practitioners, and the development of quality control systems. The project's objectives are to: establish a solid evidence base for the effective use of traditional medicine in public health; strengthen the quality, safety, appropriate use, and effective practice of traditional medicine through the regulation of products, processes, and practitioners;

translations.source: Ukukhuthaza ukuhlinzekwa kwezempilo emhlabeni wonke ngokuhlanganisa izinsiza zeT&CM ekulethweni kwezinsizakalo zokunakekelwa kwezempilo kanye nokuzinakekela. Impendulo yeWHO: Umbiko wembulunga yonke weWHO ngemithi yendabuko nehambisanayo 2019 wathuthukiswa ukuze kubhekwane negebe kudatha ethembekile, ethembekile nesemthethweni evela emazweni Angamalungu endaweni yeT&CM. Lo mbiko ubuyekeza inqubekelaphambili yomhlaba kuT&CM kule minyaka engamashumi amabili edlule futhi usekelwe emnikelweni ovela emazweni Angamalungu eWHO ayi-179. Ihlinzeka ngolwazi olubalulekile kubenzi bezinqubomgomo, ochwepheshe bezempilo kanye nomphakathi ukuze usebenzise imali engaba khona yeT&CM empilweni nasenhlalakahleni.
translations.reference: To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting universal access to healthcare by integrating T&CM into healthcare delivery and self-care services. WHO Response: The WHO's comprehensive report on traditional and complementary medicine (T&CM) for the 2019 pandemic was developed to provide reliable, evidence-based, and legally sound information from member states in the T&CM domain. The report reviews the global landscape of T&CM over the past two decades and is based on data from 179 WHO member states.
