translations.reference: The COP and CSUA bring together organizations from across sectors, income levels, and geographies, with a commitment to international cooperation and equity, and deep expertise in either genomics or country scale-up of surveillance systems. The third body is the Funders Forum to coordinate philanthropic, multi-lateral and governmental donors around increased political attention and financing efficiency of pathogen genomic surveillance. The Funders Forum also catalyses additional grant funding for projects of IPSN members. The establishment of the IPSN has been supported by German government funding to the WHO Pandemic Hub. About the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence
translations.hypothesis: COP and CSUA collaborate across all sectors, levels of government, and regions, promoting international cooperation and the sharing of best practices in public procurement. The third pillar of the Funders Forum, which brings together various funders and government officials, focuses on supporting the development and implementation of effective procurement policies. The funder's network also provides a platform for sharing examples of successful procurement projects from IPSN members. The establishment of IPSN was supported by the German WHO delegation, with the support of the WHO delegation.

translations.source: Ukuba yingxenye keWHO Health Emergencies Programme, iWHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (IHabhu yobhubhane yeWHO), izindawo zokusebenzisana kwababambisani emhlabeni abaphuma emikhakheni eminingi eyeseka amazwe kanye nababambisani ukubhekana nobhubhane lwekusasa nengcuphe ye-ephidemikhi ngokungena okungcono kumininingo namandla okuhlaziya okungcono, amathuluzi angcono nemicabango yokwenza izinqumo. Ngokwesekwa uHlulumeni weFederal Republic of Germany,iHabhu yoBhubhane yeWHO yasungulwa ngoMandulo 2021 eBerlin, ngokuphendula kubhubhane lweCOVID-19, okukhombisa ubuntekenteke emhlabeni ukuthi amazwe athola kanjani, ahlole futhi aphathe ukwesabisa kwezempilo yomphakathi. Ihabhu yoBhubhane yeWHO isebenzisana neMember States neWHO kusifunda namahhhovisi ezwe kuqinisa amanlda abo okwabelana ngemininingo,nokukwazi kwababambisani emhlabeni ukuthi basebenzisane, benze ndawonye amathuluzi nokuhlaziya idatha ngokushesha kwesixwayiso. Ngokuba khona kwamazwe angaphezu kuka-150, amahhovisi esifunda ayisithupha, nekomkhulu eliseGeneva, iWHO iyafinyelela isinikeze amandla okwelapha ubhubhane, ephidemikhi nezingcuphe zempilo yomphakathi ngokuphuthuma okulinganayo nokwenzisisa emhlabeni wonke. Ngokuxhumanisa izinyathelo zendawo, zesifunda, nezomhlaba wonke, iHabhu yoBhubhane yeWHO ikhuthaza indawo yokubambisana yabaqambi bezinto ezintsha, ososayensi kanye nochwepheshe abavela kuyo yonke imikhakha eminingi, okusivumela ukuba sisebenzise futhi sabelane ngobuchwepheshe bokusika futhi sigxilise umsebenzi wethu ezidingweni zababambiqhaza emhlabeni jikelele.
translations.reference: Forming part of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (the WHO Pandemic Hub), facilitates a global collaboration of partners from multiple sectors that supports countries and stakeholders to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, better analytical capacities, and better tools and insights for decision-making. With support from the Government of the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Pandemic Hub was established in September 2021 in Berlin, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which demonstrated weaknesses around the world in how countries detect, monitor and manage public health threats. The WHO Pandemic Hub works closely with Member States and WHO regional and country offices to strengthen their data-sharing capacities and enable partners from around the world to collaborate and co-create tools to gather and analyse data for early warning surveillance. With a presence in more than 150 countries, six regional offices, and its Geneva headquarters, WHO’s reach gives us the ability to treat pandemic, epidemic and public health risks with equal urgency and diligence around the globe. By linking local, regional, and global initiatives, the WHO Pandemic Hub fosters a collaborative environment for innovators, scientists and experts from across a wide spectrum of disciplines, allowing us to leverage and share cutting-edge technology and anchoring our work in the needs of stakeholders around the world.
translations.hypothesis: As part of the WHO's Health Emergencies Programme, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (WHO Hub) is a global platform for collaboration between various stakeholders, including governments and international organizations, to improve preparedness and response to pandemics and epidemics. By providing access to better data, tools, and expertise, the WHO Hub aims to enable more informed decision-making. Supported by the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Hub was established in Berlin in 2021 in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, highlighting the global need for improved surveillance and response capabilities. The WHO Hub works with WHO Member States and regional offices to strengthen their capacity for data sharing and collaboration, enabling a more coordinated and effective response to future health emergencies. With over 150 participating countries, regional offices, and a headquarters in Geneva, the WHO Hub provides a global platform for sharing knowledge and expertise, enabling a more rapid and coordinated response to health emergencies worldwide. By connecting local, regional, and global efforts, the WHO Hub fosters a collaborative environment for researchers, scientists, and experts from various fields, enabling us to leverage collective expertise and address global health challenges more effectively.

translations.source: Ekwakheni kulwazi olusemkhakheni yonke,iminyango,nezifunda kuzoba yisibambiso sokuhlanganisa amandla eWHO ukulondoloza izixazululo zomhlaba ukwakha kuzakhiwo ekubambisaneni komhlaba nokwethemba.
translations.reference: Building on expertise across disciplines, sectors, and regions, it will leverage WHO’s convening power to foster global solutions built on an architecture of global collaboration and trust.
translations.hypothesis: In this context, the WHO's headquarters in Geneva serves as a central hub for coordinating global efforts to address major health challenges, promote sustainable development, and foster a sense of global solidarity.

translations.source: Umhlangano weZempilo womhlaba wamashumi ayisithupha nesithupha uzogxila ekusindiseni izimpilo zabantu ukuyisa ezepilo kuwona wonke umuntu njengoba iWHO izoba na-75. Njengoba umhlaba ubhekene nokuqhubekayo komhlaba nokuphuthumayo kokuphila, uMhlangano weZempilo woMhlaba wamashumi ayisithupha nesithupha uzogxila ekuhanjisweni phambili kwezempilo kuwona wonke umuntu. Iseshini yalonyaka yeWorld Health Assembly izokhomba ikusasa lamanje nelesikhathi eside leWHO, ngokuqala ngohlelo lohlelozimali lweminyaka emibili ezayo, izinqumo ezibalulekile ngokugcineka kwezimali kweZinhlangano nezinguquko okumele zenziwe ukwenzangcono izinhlelo nezibopho zeWHO. Izihambeli zizophinde zikhululeke ngendima ebucayi yeWHO enayo eGlobal Helath Emergency Architecture. Okungale kwalezi zinkinga ukuphela konyaka wesi-75 wenhlangano,nokuvezwa kokuzuziwe okuhlangene komhlaba kusukela yasungulwa iWHO ngo-1948.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly to focus on “saving lives, driving health for all” as WHO turns 75 As the world faces ongoing health and humanitarian emergencies, the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly will focus on driving forward health for all. This year’s session of the World Health Assembly will determine the immediate and longer-term future of WHO, starting with the program budget for the next two years, key decisions about the sustainable financing of the Organization and changes put in place to improve WHO’s processes and accountability. Delegates will also deliberate about the critical role that WHO has in the Global Health Emergency Architecture. The backdrop to these issues is the organization’s 75th anniversary, with the highlights of its collective global health achievements since WHO’s creation in 1948.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Assembly, held every other year, will focus on improving the health of people worldwide, with the goal of achieving a healthy life for all by 2030, as outlined by the WHO. As the world faces ongoing health challenges and the increasing urgency of the COVID-19 pandemic, the World Health Assembly will prioritize advancing health for all. This year's session of the World Health Assembly will address the current and future challenges facing the WHO, including a two-year financing plan, key decisions on the sustainable financing of the WHO, and necessary reforms to improve the effectiveness of the WHO's programs and interventions. Participants will also discuss the WHO's role in the Global Health Emergency Architecture. The assembly will conclude with a closing ceremony, marking the 75th anniversary of the WHO's founding in 1948.

translations.source: Kuzobhekwa ukuqhubeka kwangonyaka odlule, okuzuziwe kanye nezingqinamba kanye nokuzohamba phambili ngekusasa kuzona zonke izinsika zomsebenzi weWHO:Universal Health Coverage, Okuphuthumayo, Ukugqugquzela ezempilo nokuphila kahle. WHA76 izohlangana mhlaka-21-30 kuNhlaba ePalais des Nations eGeneva. "Njengoba siqeda ukuphela kweminyaka engu-75 yeWHO singaziqhenya ngesesikuzuzile,kodwa kumele siqaphelele izifundo esesizifundile,njengoba sishintsha sisuka esigabeni esiphuthumayo seCOVID-19, futhi sakhe ikusasa lapho wonke umuntu uzothola ezempilo azidingayo," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Lokhu kusho ukuqinisa ukwakha kwezomhlaba kokuzilungiselela kwezempilo eziphuthumayo, ukuphendula nokubekezela, ukweseka amazwe endleleni eya ekukhavweni kwezempilo zomhlaba, ukwakha ngokuqina, ukugcineka ngokwezimali nokuzibophezela kweWHO. "Ukuqala iWHA76 ngeSonto mhlaka-21 kuNhlaba, uhlobo lwesine lweWalk the Talk: Ezempilo kuzona zonke izingqinamba sizoqala ePlace des Nations ngo-8:30 ekuseni CEST (ukuzilungiselela kuqala ngo-7:30 ekuseni CEST), ukugqugquzela ezempilo zesiqu nengqondo nokubungaza ukubaluleka kokubambisana ngesikhathi sokufika eminyakeni engu-75 kweWHO.
translations.reference: It will review last year’s progress, accomplishments and challenges along with future priorities across the key pillars of WHO’s work: Universal Health Coverage, Emergencies, Promoting Health and Well-being. WHA76 will convene 21-30 May at the Palais des Nations in Geneva. “As we mark WHO’s 75th anniversary, we can be proud of our past achievements, but we must be mindful of lessons learned, as we transition from the emergency phase of COVID-19, and create a future where every person has access to the health services they need,” said Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “This means strengthening the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response and resilience, supporting countries on the road to universal health coverage, and building a stronger, sustainably-funded and accountable WHO. "To kickstart WHA76 on Sunday 21 May, the fourth edition of Walk the Talk: Health for All challenge will start at Place des Nations at 8:30 am CEST (warm-up starts at 7:30 am CEST), to promote physical and mental health and celebrate the importance of partnership during the WHO’s 75th anniversary.
translations.hypothesis: The ongoing challenges and uncertainties of the past year, as well as the future prospects for all of the WHO's key health priorities: Universal Health Coverage, Emergency Health Response, Health Promotion, and Well-being, will be addressed at the 76th World Health Assembly, which will take place from 21 to 30 June at the Palais des Nations in Geneva. "As we conclude the 75th year of the WHO, we can take pride in our achievements, but we must also remain vigilant, as we transition from the COVID-19 emergency to a new era where everyone has access to the health services they need," said Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "This means strengthening the global architecture for emergency health preparedness, response, and resilience, and ensuring that the WHO has the resources and capacity to meet the challenges ahead. The 76th WHA will begin on Sunday, 21 June, with the opening ceremony at 8:30 am CEST (7:30 am CEST), followed by a series of plenary sessions and side events at the Palais des Nations.

translations.source: Kuyizikhathi zo-2:00 ntamabama CEST, kulandela isithombe somndeni nezinhloko zezihambeli, uMhlangano wezinga eliphezulu wamukela ePalais des Nations kuzoba nezinkulumo eziphuma kuMqondisi Jikelele weWHO Izinhloko zamazwe kanye nezihambeli ezikhethekile. Uhla lwezikhulumi ngezansi). Ngesikhathi seseshini, iDG izokwethula umklomelo wesikhathi eside kubaholi ababili bezempilo emhlabeni. NgoMsombuluko mhlaka-22 kuNhlaba, uMqondisi Jikelele uzokwethula inkulumo yakhe ebalulekile esikhathini esihlawumbiselwa lu-10:00 ekuseni. NgoLwesihlanu, mhlaka-26 kuNhlaba, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO uzojoyinwa ngabamele izisekelo zomhlaba ezinhlanu,ukumela imiklomelo yezempilo yomphakathi emihlanu kanye nemiklomelo kwabahlonishiwe ngemiklomelo abayisikhombisa ngo-2023. Baqhamuka eBahrain, eChina, eGuatemala, eLebanon, eNepal, eThailand kanye nePhilippines.
translations.reference: At about 2:00 pm CEST, following a family photo with Heads of Delegations, the Assembly high-level welcome at Palais des Nations will feature speeches from the WHO Director-General, Heads of State and special guests. List of speakers below). During this session, the DG will present life-time achievement awards to two global health leaders. On Monday 22 May, the Director-General will give his main address at approximately 10:00 am. On Friday, 26 May, the WHO Director-General will be joined by representatives of five global foundations, to present five public health awards and prizes to the seven laureates for 2023. They come from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand and the Philippines.
translations.hypothesis: The meeting will take place at 2:00 PM CEST, following a family photo and introductions of the speakers. The high-level meeting will be held at the Palais des Nations and will include presentations from the Director-General of the WHO, as well as representatives from specific countries and organizations. A list of speakers is below. During the session, the DG will present a long-term vision for global health leadership. On Wednesday, November 22nd, the Director-General will deliver a keynote speech at 10:00 AM. On Thursday, November 23rd, the Director-General of the WHO will be honored with five global health awards, as well as five community health awards and a special award from the 2023 laureates. The recipients will be from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand, and the Philippines.

translations.source: Izinkinga ezibalulekile Izingxoxo ezibalulekile kanye nezinqumo kuzolindeleka: Ukubhekwa komsebenzi weWHO kokuphuhumayo kwezempilo okufaka uKulawulwa kwezempilo emazweni angaphandle kanye nokuqinisa ukuzilungiselela kweWHO futhi nokuphendula okuphuthumayo kwezempilo; Amasu nezenzo zomhlaba ezindaweni njengabesifazane nezingane nezempilo yentsha,ukuvuselela isimilo, ukukhavwa kwezempilo komhlaba, nokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala, imithi yesintu, ukuvikela ukutheleleka nokulawula izinga eliphansi nemithi engumgunyathi, ezempilo zabangenandawo nabafudukile, izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo, ezempilo yengqondo ukuzimisela ngenhlalakahle, ezokudla kanye nokukhubazeka; Ukugunyazwa koHlelo loHlelomali lweWHO lwango-2024-2025 okubandakanya izinqumo ukunyusa ukubhekwa kweminikelo kanyenezinye izindaba eziqhamuka kuWorking Group on Sustainable Financing.
translations.reference: Key issues Key discussions and decisions are expected on: A review of WHOs work in health emergencies, including the International Health Regulations and strengthening WHO preparedness for and response to health emergencies; Strategies and global action on areas such as women’s, children’s and adolescents’ health, rehabilitation, universal health coverage and primary health care, traditional medicine, infection prevention and control, substandard and falsified medicines, health of refugees and migrants, non-communicable diseases, mental health, social determinants, nutrition and disabilities; Approval of the WHO Programme Budget for 2024-2025, including the decision to increase assessed contributions and other matters emanating from the Working Group on Sustainable Financing.
translations.hypothesis: Key challenges and priorities include: Strengthening WHO's core functions in health, including global health governance, pandemic preparedness and response, and health system strengthening; Addressing health inequities in areas such as maternal and child health, non-communicable diseases, and mental health; Implementing the WHO's 2024-2025 financing strategy, including decisions on increasing funding for priority areas.

translations.source: Amatafula amasu ansukuzonke azokwenzeka ngesikhathi sesidlo sasemini kusukela ngoMsombuluko kuya NgoMgqibelo egumbini XVIII. Le micimbi izobamba izingxoxo kuzihambeli zomhlangano, ababambisani bama-ejensi, abamele umphakathi kanye nongoti beWHO kokuhamba phambili kwezempilo yomphakathi. Amaseshini azokwenzeka kusuka ngomhlaka-22-27 kuNhlaba kusuka ngo-13:00 kuya ku-14:15 CEST futhi kungalandelwa ku-inthanethi. Njengebhodi elithatha izinqumo ezinkulu leWHO uMhlangano weZempilo uhlele inqubomgomo yeNhlangano futhi yagunyaza uhlelomali lwayo. I-WHA yethanyelwe yizihambeli eziphuma kuWHO 194 Member States.
translations.reference: Daily Strategic roundtables will take place at lunch time from Monday to Saturday in Room XVIII. These events will host discussions among assembly delegates, partner agencies, representatives of civil society and WHO experts on public health priorities. Sessions run from 22-27 May, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST and can be followed online. As WHO’s highest decision-making body, the Health Assembly sets out the Organization’s policy and approves its budget. WHA is attended by delegations from all WHO 194 Member States.
translations.hypothesis: The annual World Health Assembly, which takes place from Monday to Friday each year during the XVIII General Assembly of the World Health Organization, will bring together delegates from member states, representatives from various organizations, and WHO officials to discuss and advance public health. The sessions will take place from Monday, 22 to Friday, 27 July, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST, and will be available online. The World Health Assembly is the main decision-making body of the WHO, and it sets the organization's priorities and approves its budget. The WHA is attended by delegates from the 194 member states of the WHO.

translations.source: Ajenda yoMhlangano weZempilo uqalwe ngomhlangano wangomhlaka-30 wohlelo, uhlelomali kanye neKomidi eliphethe lebhodi elikhulu,elizohlangana ngomhlaka-17-19 kuNhlaba 2023. Ngemva komhlangano umhlangano wesi-153 webhodi labaphathi uzokwenzeka kusuka ngomhlaka-31 kuNhlaba kuya mhlaka-1 kuNhlangulana. Umhlangano weZempilo uvulelekile kwababhalisa kanye nabagunyaziwe beMember State, Associate Members, abazobuka nabazomela abamenyiwe be-UN kanye nabanye abazobamba iqhaza bezinhlangano zikahulumeni nemikhakha okungeyona ekahulumeni. I-WHA76 izokwethula ku-inthanethi kuwebhusayithi yeWHO. Ajenda yesikhashana, uhla ngesikhathi,amadokhumenti omhlangano kanye nohambo losuku kungatholakala lapha. Ukuqinisekiswa kwabezindaba kanye nokubuyekezwa kweWorld Health Assembly
translations.reference: The Health Assembly’s agenda is preceded by the 30th meeting of the Programme, Budget and Administration Committee of the Executive Board, which will meet on 17-19 May, 2023. After the Assembly, the 153th meeting of the Executive Board will take place from 31 May to 1 June. The Health Assembly is open to registered and accredited Member States, Associate Members, Observers, invited representatives of the UN and other participating inter-governmental organizations and non-State actors. WHA76 will be webcast live from the WHO website. The provisional agenda, preliminary timetable, Assembly documents and daily journal can be accessed here.Media accreditation and updates for the World Health Assembly
translations.hypothesis: The Health Assembly meeting began with a session on the 30th of the month, involving the executive board, the committee, and the main board, which will take place on the 17th-19th of November 2023. Following the session, the 153rd meeting of the executive board will take place from the 31st of the month to the 1st of December. The Health Assembly is open to registered and invited members of Member States, Associate Members, UN delegates, and other participants in government and non-governmental organizations. The WHA76 will be available online on the WHO website. A provisional agenda, a timetable, documents for the meeting, and travel information can be found here.

translations.source: Ukugunyazwa kwabezindaba kweWHA76 kuzophathwa yi-United Nations yaseGeneva. Bhalisa ku-Indico. Ukulanda ibheji lakho: NgoLwesihlanu mhlaka-19 kuNhlaba (amahora okusebenza: u-9:00 ekuseni kuya ku-16:30 ntambama CEST) eVilla Feuillantines, Place des Nations. Imininingwane kanye nekheli: http://unece.orgimortant-information-hoe-to-obtain-your-badge NgoMgqibelo 20 Nhlaba: UN Palais des Nations ePregny Gate (maqondana nesakhiwo se-ICRC) kusuka ngo-13:00 kuya ku-17:00 CEST. NgeSonto mhlaka 21 kuNhlaba kumele ulande ibheji labezindaba e-UN Palais des Nations ePregny Gate (maqondana nesakhiwo se-ICRC) kusuka ngo-9:00 ekuseni kuya ku-16:00 ntambama CEST
translations.reference: Media accreditation for WHA76 will be handled by the United Nations of Geneva. Register via Indico. To collect your badge: On Friday 19 May (working hours: 9:00 am to 16:30 pm CEST) at Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations . Details and address : https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge On Saturday 20 May: UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (opposite ICRC Building) from 13:00 to 17:00 CEST. On Sunday 21 May, you shall collect your media badge at the UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (opposite ICRC Building) from 9:00am to 16:00 pm CEST
translations.hypothesis: The distribution of WHA76 press passes will be handled by the United Nations office in Geneva. Registration is required on Indico. To obtain your press pass: On Wednesday, November 19th (working hours: 9:00 am to 4:30 pm CEST), at Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations. For more information and address: http://unece.org/important-information-how-to-obtain-your-badge On Monday, November 20th: UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC building) from 1:00 pm to 5:00 pm CEST. On Sunday, November 21st, you must obtain your press pass at UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC building) from 9:00 am to 4:00 pm CEST.

translations.source: Ngesonto elizayo - mhlaka-22-26 kuNhlaba- ukulanda amabheji ezindaba: eVilla Les Feuillantines ePlace des Nations. Ukubuyekezwa koMhlangano weZempilo kuzofakwa kuwebhusayithi yeWHO kanye nezinye izinto ezihlobene zizothunyelwa ohlweni lwabezindaba zomhlaba. Bhalisela ukubuyekezwa. Izithombe namavidiyo Izithombe zeWHO zikhona kwabezindaba zisebenzise ngokungena kumtapolwazi wezithombe weWHO.
translations.reference: Next week - 22-26 May- to collect the media badges: Villa Les Feuillantines at Place des Nations. Updates on the Health Assembly will be posted on the WHO website and relevant materials will be sent to the global media list. Register for updates. Photographs and videos WHO photos are available for media use via the WHO photo library.
translations.hypothesis: Next week – from November 22nd to 26th – news outlets will be covering: The World Health Organization's (WHO) Health Assembly, held at the Villa Les Feuillantines in Place des Nations. A summary of the assembly's key findings will be published on the WHO website, along with other related information, and will be sent to major news organizations. Subscribe to receive the summary. Images and videos: Images and videos from the WHO are available for use by news organizations through the WHO's image library.

translations.source: Bhalisa kanye ukungena kwabezindaba kumtapolwazi wezithombe zeWHO ngokugcindezela, "ukubhaliswa kwezindaba" kulelikhasi. Imibuzo ehlobene nezithombe zeWHOimeyili [imeyili ivikeliwe] ividiyo yokusakaza iqoshiwe ngezinto kanye nokunye kwezindaba okuqukethwe kuzobakhona kwabezindaba ukuba bakulande kuCanto. Noma yimiphi imibzo ehlobene nokudluliselwa kokubukwa kwevidiyo sicela uthumele imeyili [imeyili ivikelwe]. Izinkundla zokuxhumana, iWHO izonikeza ngemininingwane njalo ephuma kuWorld Health Assembly ngoTwitter -landela @WHO nehashtag #WHA76. Landela iWHO kwezinye izinkundla zokuxhumana ngokubuyekeziwe #WHA76: kuFacebook, Instagram, Linkedin, YouTube.
translations.reference: Register once for media access to the WHO photo library by clicking “media registration” on this page. For questions related to WHO photos email [email protected] Recorded broadcast video material and other press content will be available for media to download on Canto. For any questions related to live video transmission please email [email protected]. Social mediaWHO will provide regular updates from the World Health Assembly via Twitter –follow @WHO and hashtag #WHA76.Follow WHO’s other social media accounts for updates from #WHA76: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube
translations.hypothesis: To subscribe to and receive WHO image updates, click "subscribe" in this section. For inquiries related to WHO images, please email [email protected]. Video demonstrations of how to use the WHO image library and other relevant news are available. Please email [email protected] for any inquiries related to video distribution. The WHO will provide regular updates from the World Health Assembly on Twitter - follow @WHO and the hashtag #WHA76. Follow the WHO on other social media platforms with the updated #WHA76: on Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, and YouTube.

translations.source: Ukwenza okuphuthumayo kudinga kubhekwane nakho kulokhu kumiswa kokuqhubeka kokuhlobene nezempilo kwe Sustainable Development Goals I-WHO izokhipha uhlobo lwa-2023 lombiko wonyaka weWorld Health Statistics nezibalo ezintsha kumthelela wobhubhane leCOVID-19 kanye nezibalo zakamuva ekuqhubekeni okuya kokuhlobene nezempilo zeSustainable Development Goals (amaSDG). Umbiko wemininingo engafika ku-2022 ekala ukuma kokuqhubeka kwezempilo kuzinkomba zezempilo ezibalulekile eminyakeni yakamuva uma uqhathanisa nokudumile okubonakele ngesikhathi sika-2000-2015. Kuphinde kuqaphelise kuthina ukukhula kokusabisa kwezifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo (amaNCD) nokuguquka kwesimo sezulu nezingcingo zokuxhumana nokuqinisa impendulo. I-COVID-19 ibiza izimpilo ezilahlekile nokuqhubeka kwezempilo amadokhumenti ombiko yezibalo ezibuyekeziwe esiqongweni sobhubhane lezempilo yomhlaba,ukunikela ekuqhubekeni kokwehla kokuqhubeka okuya kumaSDG.
translations.reference: Urgent action needed to tackle stalled progress on health-related Sustainable Development Goals WHO is releasing the 2023 edition of its annual World Health Statistics report with new figures on the impact of COVID-19 pandemic and the latest statistics on progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report with data up to 2022 underscores a stagnation of health progress on key health indicators in recent years compared with trends seen during 2000-2015. It also alerts us to the growing threat of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) and climate change, and calls for a coordinated and strengthened response. COVID-19 cost in lost lives and health progress The report documents updated statistics on the toll of the pandemic on global health, contributing to the ongoing decline in progress towards the SDGs.
translations.hypothesis: Taking immediate action is crucial to address the ongoing challenges to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals. The WHO will release its 2023 edition of the World Health Statistics report, along with new data on the COVID-19 pandemic and recent developments in the area of sustainable development. The report includes data from up to 2022, and highlights the continued progress made in several key health indicators over the past decade, compared to the baseline period of 2000-2015. It also addresses the growing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs), as well as the impacts of climate change and the need for increased investment in health systems. The COVID-19 pandemic has had a significant impact on health and development, and the report provides updated data to inform efforts to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals.

translations.source: Ngesikhathi sa-2020-2021 iCOVID-19 ilethe ukuhamba kancane izigidi ezingu-336.8 eminyakeni yempilo elahlekile emhlabeni. Lokhu kulinganisa isilinganiso seminyaka engu-22 yempilo elahlekile kukhona konke ukufa okwenzekile, ubunzima bokusheshe uqede izimpilo eziyizigidi zabantu. Kusukela ngo-2000, sibone ukuthuthuka okukhulu kwezempilo zomama nezingane nokushona okwehla ikota eyodwa nohhafu, ngokulandelana. Isigameko sezifo ezithelelanayo njengeHIV, isifo sofuba (TB) kanye nesifo sikamalaleveva siphinde sehla kanye nokwehla kwengcuphe yokufa kwabasheshe bazalwa eNCD kanye nokulimala. Ndawonye, lokhu kube nomthelela ekunyukeni kwesikhathi sokuphila emhlabeni kusuka ku-67 iminyaka ngo-2000 kuya ku-73 iminyaka ngo-2019.
translations.reference: During 2020-2021, COVID-19 resulted in a staggering 336.8 million years of life lost globally. This equates to an average of 22 years of life lost for every excess death, abruptly and tragically cutting short the lives of millions of people. Since 2000, we saw significant improvements in maternal and child health with deaths falling by one-third and one-half, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis (TB) and malaria also declined, along with a lowered risk of premature deaths from NCDs and injuries. Together, these contributed to an increase in global life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.
translations.hypothesis: During the 2020-2021 period, the COVID-19 pandemic caused an estimated 336.8 million years of healthy life lost globally. This represents a decline of approximately 22 years in average healthy life expectancy, highlighting the devastating impact of the pandemic on global health. Since 2000, there has been significant progress in maternal and child health and a decline in mortality rates for specific diseases, such as HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria. However, progress has stalled in other areas, including non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and injuries. As a result, the average global life expectancy has declined from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.

translations.source: Kepha ke, ubhubhane selubeke izinkomba eziningi ezihlobene nezempilo ukuqhubeka ukuchezuka nokunikela ekungalinganeni kokuthola ukunakekelwa kwezempilo okusezingeni eliphezulu, izindlela zokugoma kanye nokuvikelwa kwezomphakathi. Njengomphumela, ukwenzangcono okudumile kwamalaleveva neTB sekuhlehlisiwe nabantu abambalwa balashwa belashelwa izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe (amaNTD). "I-World Health Statistics kuwukubheka kweminyaka kweWHO esimweni sezempilo yomhlaba. Umbiko uthumela umyalezo ekwesabeni izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,okuthatha okukhulu nokunyuka ezimpilweni zabantu,ukuphila,izinhlelo zempilo ,imiphakathi ezomnotho nemiphakathi," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Izimemezelo zombiko zokunyuka okungagcineka kutshalomali lwezempilo ukubuyela esimweni seSustainable Development Goals."
translations.reference: However, the pandemic has put many health-related indicators further off-track and contributed to inequalities in access to high-quality health care, routine immunizations and financial protection. As a result, improving trends in malaria and TB have been reversed, and fewer people were treated for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). “The World Health Statistics is WHO’s annual check-up on the state of the world’s health. The report sends a stark message on the threat of noncommunicable diseases, which take an immense and increasing toll on lives, livelihoods, health systems, communities, economies and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The report calls for a substantial increase in investments in health and health systems to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals.”
translations.hypothesis: However, the pandemic has also highlighted the importance of public health, including access to quality healthcare, disease prevention and control, and social protection. As a result, there has been a renewed focus on addressing the underlying causes of infectious diseases, such as poverty and inequality. "The World Health Statistics report provides a comprehensive overview of the global health situation. It includes data on the prevalence of infectious diseases, as well as information on health systems, health financing, and social determinants of health," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. "The report also highlights the need for increased investment in public health to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals."

translations.source: Ama-NCD-ahlezi enyusa ukwesabela ezempilo zesizukulwane sakusasa, Ngaphandle kwakho konke ukuqhubeka, ukwabelana ngokufa okudalwe ngonyaka amaNCD sekukhule njalo futhi manje sekuthathe amakota amathathu azo zonke izimpilo ezilahlekile unyaka ngamunye. Uma lokhu kujwayeleka kuqhubeka, amaNCD azothatha icala le-86% wokufa kwezigidi ezingu-90 ngonyaka phakathi kweminyaka eyikhulu, ngokulandelana izigidi ezingu-77 zalokhu ngezeNCD -kungafinyelela ku-90% ukwenyuka kwezinamba kusukela ngo-2019. Ukuhamba kancane kokuqhubeka kwezimemezelo zokuqhubeka okudumile kwakamuva kukhombisa izinkomba zokuhamba kancane kwezinga lonyaka lokwehla kwezinkomba eziningi (ARR). Isibonelo isilinganiso sokufa komama emhlabeni sidinga ukwehla ngo-11.6% ngonykaka phakathi kuka-2021 no-2030 ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe yiSDG. Ngokufanayo, ukwehla konke endabeni yeTB kusuka ngo-2015 kuya ku-2021 kwakungamakota amahlanu kosekuhanjiwe ngo-2025 kweWHO END TB Strategy.
translations.reference: NCDs ̶ an ever-increasing health threat for future generationsDespite overall health progress, the share of deaths caused annually by NCDs has grown consistently and is now claiming nearly three quarters of all lives lost each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for about 86% of the 90 million annual deaths by mid-century; consequently, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a nearly 90% increase in absolute numbers since 2019. Stagnating progress calls for acceleration More recent trends show signs of slowdown in the annual rate of reduction (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality ratio needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Similarly, the net reduction in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-fifth of the way to the 2025 milestone of WHO’s End TB Strategy.
translations.hypothesis: Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) pose a significant threat to the health of future generations. In addition to the ongoing increase in the prevalence of NCDs, the number of deaths caused by these diseases has also been steadily rising, with an estimated 41 million deaths occurring each year due to NCDs. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for 86% of all deaths globally by 2030, compared to the current 77% in 2019. The continued rise in prevalence of these common NCDs indicates a downward trend in the overall mortality rate. For example, the global mortality rate for heart disease is projected to decline by 11.6% between 2021 and 2030, in line with the WHO's End TB Strategy.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kokwehla ekubonakaleni kwengcuphe yezempilo eningi-njengokusetshenziswa kwagwayi, ukuphuza utshwala, udlame,amanzi angaphephile nokuhlanzeka, okwezingane, ukuqhubeka nokunganeli nokuvuleleka kwezinye izingcuphe njengokungcola komoya kuhlezi kuphezulu. Okuthusayo, ukusabalala kokukhuluphala ngokweqile kuyanyuka kungekho nophawu lokukubuyisela emuva. Ukuqhubekaukwanda kokuthola emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile sekwehlile uma uqhathanisa kokuzuza kwaphambili kwa-2015, okuhlanganiswe nokuqhubeka okukhulu ekwehliseni ubunzima kwezezimali ngenxa yokubiza kokunakekelwa kwezempilo. Imikhawulo emikhulu yokukwazi kwethu ukuthola i-Universal Health Coverage ngo-2030. "Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 luyisikhumbuzo esibalulekile sokuthi ukuqhubeka akuhambi ngolayini noma akuqinisekisiwe," kuxwayisa uDkt Samira Asma, Umsizi woMqondisi Jikele weMininingo kuWHO, ukuhlaziya nokulethwa kwemithelela.
translations.reference: Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact.
translations.hypothesis: Beyond the decline in access to essential health services such as vaccinations, prenatal care, and safe water and sanitation, there is a worrying trend of increasing inequality in health outcomes. Specifically, the gap between the wealthiest and poorest segments of the population is widening. Furthermore, the rate of progress towards achieving universal health coverage has slowed significantly compared to the gains made before 2015, and is threatened by ongoing financial constraints and challenges in delivering care. Significant barriers remain to achieving universal health coverage by 2030. "The COVID-19 pandemic is a stark reminder that progress is not guaranteed and that continued efforts are essential," warns Dr. Samira Asma, Deputy Director of the WHO's Department of Health and Environment, emphasizing the need for sustained investment and innovation.

translations.source: "Ukuhlala esimweni okuya kuAjenda yeSDG 2030, kumele sinqume kanhle silethe ngokubambisana kumthelela okaliwe kuwona wonke amazwe." Umbiko wakulonyaka ufaka okokuqala isahluko sokuzinikela kwezokuguquka kwesimo sezulu nezempilo, futhi siyakubona lokhu kuzohlobana embikweni kusuka manje kuya phambili. Ngalenkinga nazo zonke izindawo,ukwethembeka kanye nokungesona isilinganisso solwazi kubucayi ukulandelela ukuqhubeka nokwenza ngcono ezweni nakuzinqubomgomo zezempilo emhlabeni. Inothi lomhleli: iWolrd Health Statistics umbiko yiWolrd Health Organisation (WHO) ukuhlanganisa konyaka kolwazi olukhona lwakamuva kwezempilo kanye nezinkomba ezihlobene nezempilo. Uma unemibuzo xhumana [imeyili ivikelekile]
translations.reference: “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]
translations.hypothesis: "Living in accordance with the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals, we must make informed decisions and work together to achieve the targets set by all countries. This annual report marks the beginning of a series on climate change and health, and we expect to see this theme continue in future reports. In this report, we highlight the importance of trust and transparency in tracking progress and improving health outcomes globally. Editor's note: The World Health Statistics report is published by the World Health Organization (WHO) and includes the latest available data on health and related indicators. If you have any questions, please contact [protected email address]

translations.source: Umqondisi Jikelele weWHO umemezele Imiklomelo yaBaholi beZempilo eMhlabeni UJean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum noPeter Piot baklonyelwe ngokuzibophezela impilo yabo yonke kwezempilo Umqondisi Jikelele weWHO uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uzokwethula imiklomelo yempilo yonke emibili ukubona ukunikela okumangazayo ukwenza ngcono ezempilo zomhlaba ngesikhathi seseshini yokuvula esezingeni eliphakeme yoMhlangano weZempilo woMhlaba wamashumi ayisithupha nesithupha. Imiklomelo yaBaholi boMhlaba yaMqondisi Jikelele, eyasungulwa ngo-2019 izoya kuJean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum noPeter Piot. Bobabili abatholile bangosayensi abahlukile futhi bangabaholi bezempilo yomphakathi ababezibandakanye kakhulu ekutholeni isifo segciwane ibhola ngaphambi kokudlulela phambili ngesikhundla kwezobuholi kwezempilo emhlabeni.
translations.reference: WHO Director-General announces Global Health Leaders Awards Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot awarded for lifelong commitment to health WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus will present two lifetime achievement awards to recognize outstanding contributions to advancing global health during the high-level opening session of the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly. The Director-General’s Global Leaders Awards, which were established in 2019, will go to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. Both recipients are distinguished scientists and public health leaders who were closely involved in the discovery of Ebola virus disease before advancing to leadership positions in global health.
translations.hypothesis: The Director-General of the WHO, Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, has announced that the honorary titles of "Global Champions of Health" will be awarded to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot in recognition of their outstanding contributions to global health. The honorary titles will be presented at the opening ceremony of the 74th World Health Assembly, which will take place in Geneva next month. The Global Champions of Health award, established in 2019, recognizes individuals who have made a significant contribution to improving global health. Muyembe-Tamfum and Piot are both renowned scientists and public health leaders who have made significant contributions to the fight against HIV/AIDS.

translations.source: Umklomelo ubahlonipha ngabakuzuze impilo yabo yonke kwezempilo yomphakathi. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum waseDemocratic Republic of Congo (DRC) uyabonwa ngokwenza okusha, ubuholi bakhe nomnikelo omangazayo kuya ekuphatheni nokwelapha izifo ezinjenge sifo segciwane i-Ebola. Usedlale indima ebucayi ekwenzeni ngcono imiphumela yezempilo eDemocratic Republic of Congo, isifunda sase-Afrika kanye nomphakathi wasemhlabeni. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum unguMqondisi Jikelele weDRC National Institute of Biomedical Research eKinshasa, uSolwazi ogcwele weMicrobiology eNyuvesi yaseKinshasa Medical School nomongameli wokwethula waseCongolese Academy of Science. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum wayeyingxenye yeqembu elathola ukuthi iZaire inegciwane lebhola ngesikhathi kuqoshwa okokuqala ukubheduka ngo-1976 eSifundazweni saseEquateur, Maphakathi neDRC.
translations.reference: The award honours their lifetime achievements in public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being recognized for his innovation, leadership and ground-breaking contributions to the management and treatment of diseases such as Ebola virus disease. He has played a critical role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the African region, and the global community. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the DRC’s National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, full Professor of Microbiology at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and the inaugural president of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that discovered the Zaïre ebolavirus during the first recorded outbreak in 1976, in Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum is highly respected for his contributions to public health. As a leading expert in infectious diseases, he has made significant contributions to the prevention and treatment of diseases such as Ebola. He has played a crucial role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of Congo, as well as in Africa and around the world. Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director of the DRC National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, a Professor of Microbiology at the Kinshasa Medical School, and the President of the Congolese Academy of Science. He was also a member of the team that first identified the Ebola virus in Zaire in 1976, during the initial screening of samples from the Equateur province.

translations.source: Makhathaleni waseza ukwakha omunye wemishanguzo youqala esebenzayo ekulweni negciwane nokufakwa kokuhlolwa kwemigomo yesifo segciwane i-Ebola. Usesebenza ngokungakhathali ukwakha iDemocratic Republic of the Congo emandleni esayensi, ukwakha izindawo zokwenza ucwaningo oluningi ezweni, usedlale indima ebaluleke kakhulu neWHO ekulweni nezifo ezithelelanayo. USolwazi Piot waseBelgium uyabonakala ngobuholi obuhle kakhulu ekuphenduleni ngobhubhane lweHIV/AIDS kanye nezinye izingqinamba ezibalulekile zezempilo emhlabeni. Usedlale indima ebaluleke kakhulu ekwethulweni kwemizamo emikhulu yezempilo emhlabeni njenge-UNAIDS, iGlobal Fund, kanye neminye esiyenze ngcono imiphumela yezempilo emhlabeni wonke. Ngaphansi kobuholi bakhe, i-UNAIDS ibe ngabameli abakhulu emhlabeni wonke bokwenza okubhekene neAIDS kanye nokukhomba indlela ekwenzeni busha i-UN ngokuhlanganisa ndawonye izinhlangano ezahlukene ezingu-10 ze-UN.
translations.reference: He later helped design one of the first effective treatments against the virus and the deployment of experimental Ebola virus disease vaccines. He has worked tirelessly to build the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s scientific capacity, establishing multiple research facilities in the country, and has played key roles with WHO in fighting infectious diseases. Professor Piot from Belgium is being recognized for his extraordinary leadership in the response to the HIV/AIDS epidemic and other significant global health challenges. He has played a vital role in launching major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others that have improved health outcomes around the world. Under his leadership, UNAIDS became the chief advocate for worldwide action against AIDS, also spearheading UN reform by bringing together 10 different UN organizations.
translations.hypothesis: Makhathaleni was instrumental in establishing one of the first operational research centers for the diagnosis and treatment of Ebola and other tropical diseases. He played a crucial role in strengthening the Democratic Republic of the Congo's scientific capacity, establishing research facilities, and collaborating with the WHO on the control of infectious diseases. Dr. Piot of Belgium has been a leading figure in the global response to HIV/AIDS and other major public health challenges. He has been instrumental in the development of major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and other efforts to improve health outcomes worldwide. Under his leadership, UNAIDS has become a leading global organization working to end the AIDS epidemic and to promote the health of the entire UN system by bringing together ten different UN agencies.

translations.source: USolwazi Piot ube yingxenye futhi yokusheshe kutholakale igciwane i-Ebola, nokulilawula ngesikhathi libheduka okokuqala lingaziwa, ukubamba iqhaza ekwenzeni ucwaningo ngezobhubhane. Usebe ngusihlalo wemizamo eminingi esezingeni eliphezulu,wasebenza emazingeni aphezulu amaningi njengomeluleki, okufaka nakuCOVID-19 usethole imiklomelo eminingi. USolwazi Piot unguMeluleki okhethekile kuMongameli wase-EC, uSolwazi weZempilo yoMhlaba owayenguMqondisi weLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. "Laba bantu abahlabahlosile banokuzinikela impilo yonke, ukumela, ukuzibophezela ekulinganeni, ukusebenzela abantu kanye nezempilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngesikhathi sokuphuthumayo okuningi,izinkinga zezempilo nokuxabana,siyabubona ubuholi kwezempilo yomphakathi nesayensi kubaluleke kakhulu ukwenzangcono ezempilo emhlabeni.
translations.reference: Professor Piot also played a part in the earlier discovery of the Ebola virus, and the containment of the first known outbreak, participating in field epidemiological investigations. He has chaired a number of high-level initiatives, served in numerous high-level advisory roles, including on COVID-19, and has received multiple awards. Professor Piot is the Special Advisor to the EC President, a Professor of Global Health and former Director of the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. “These outstanding individuals embody lifelong dedication, relentless advocacy, a commitment to equity, and selfless service of humanity and people’s health,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “In a time of multiple emergencies, health crises, and conflict, we recognize that leadership in public health and science are more important than ever to advance global health.
translations.hypothesis: Professor Piot has been a leading figure in the fight against Ebola, and his expertise in managing outbreaks during their initial stages has been invaluable. He has also been involved in numerous high-level initiatives, including advising on the response to COVID-19. Professor Piot is a Special Advisor to the Director-General of the WHO, and he is also a member of the Executive Board of the World Health Organization. "These individuals are essential to the health of everyone, they provide leadership, expertise, and support to help us address the challenges facing global health," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "In times of crisis, we need strong leadership and collaboration to ensure that we are doing everything we can to protect the health of people around the world.

translations.source: Abahlonishwa bethu namhlanje bakhombisa leyo ntshisekelo." "Izindaba zabo behlangene nokuphumelela kukhombisa ukuthi umhlaba usuqhubeke kangakanani ngokokubambisana emhlabeni,esifundeni nasemazingeni ezwe ukuqinisa ukuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo kwezempilo nokuphendula nokubekezela. Amasayensisi agqamile azoqhubeka ukugqugquzela zonke izinsuku," wengezile emcimbini wemiklomelo njengengxenye yeseshini yokuvula esezingeni eliphezulu lesi-76th World Health Assembly ozobanjwa ngesonto mhlaka-21 May 2023 eGeneva.
translations.reference: Our awardees today personify that passion.” “Their combined stories and successes show how much the world has progressed in terms of collaboration at the global, regional and country levels to strengthen health emergency preparedness, response and resilience. These outstanding scientists will continue to inspire us every day,” he added The ceremony for the awards form part of the high-level opening session of the 76th World Health Assembly being held on Sunday 21 May 2023 in Geneva.
translations.hypothesis: Our distinguished guests have highlighted this important issue. "Their experiences and success demonstrate that the world is still facing significant challenges, particularly in areas such as global health security, pandemic preparedness, and response. Dedicated scientists will continue to play a vital role in addressing these challenges," she stated at the awards ceremony as part of the opening session of the 76th World Health Assembly, which will be held in Geneva on May 21, 2023.

translations.source: I-WHO ihalalisela iBenin neMali ngokuqeda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi I-WHO isiqinisekise iBenin neMali ngokuqda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi,nokubenza abesihlanu nesithupha amazwe aseAfrika eWHO ukuthola umsebenzi omkhulu kangaka. Amazwe asethole ukugunyazwa okuvela kwiWHO ngokuqeda isifo samehlo yiGhana (ngoNhlangulana 2018) iGambia (ngoMbasa 2021) Togo (ngoNhlaba 2022 kanye neMalawi (ngoMandulo 2022). "WHO ihalalisela abasemagunyeni bezempilo baseBenin neMali kanye nohlelokuxhumana lwabo emhlabeni kanye nababambisani bendawo kulokhu asebekuzuzile," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Kulandela impumelelo yeBenin neMali isifo samehlo siyaqhubeka siba wubhubhane emazweni angu-23 eWHO African Region, kusiletha eduze kokuqedwa okuhlosiwe okuhleliwe kwesifo samehlo kubalazwe kulezofifo ezijwaelekile ezinganakiwe 2021-2030."
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.”
translations.hypothesis: The WHO has praised Benin and Mali for their efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health priority. The WHO has recognized Benin and Mali for their progress in eliminating trachoma as a public health priority, making them the fifth and sixth countries in Africa to achieve this milestone. The two countries received recognition from the WHO for their efforts to eliminate trachoma from Ghana (in 2018), The Gambia (in 2021), Togo (in 2022), and Malawi (in 2022). "I am pleased to commend the health ministries of Benin and Mali, as well as their partners, for their dedication to this important cause," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "Following the success of Benin and Mali, trachoma is on track to be eliminated as a public health problem in the WHO African Region by 2021, as outlined in the new Global Elimination Goal for trachoma 2021-2030.

translations.source: Emhlabeni iBenin neMali bazojoyina amanye amazwe angu-15 asegunyazwe yiWHO ngokuqeda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi. Lawo mazwe yiCambodia, iChina, iGambia, iGhana, i-Islamic Republic of Iran, iLao Peoples Democratic Republic, iMalawi, iMexico, iMorocco, iMyanmar, iNepal, i-Oman, iSaudi Arabia, iTogo neVanuatu. Ukuqeda isifo samehlo usebenzisa isu leSAFE leWHO Womabili amazwe iBenin neMali benze isiphakamiso sesu lokuphepha leWHO ukuqeda isifo samehlo ngokwesekwa yiWHO nababambisani. Isu leSAFE liqukethe ukuhlinzwa ukuze welaphe isifo samehlo ngobunzia,ama-antibhayothikhi ukuqeda ukungcola,izinto zokuhlanza ubuso,ukwenzangcono indawo,ikakhulukazi ukwenza ngcono ukuthola amazi nokuthuthwa kwendle ukwehlisa ukudlulisela.
translations.reference: Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission.
translations.hypothesis: In Benin and Mali, along with 15 other countries identified by the WHO, are working to eliminate trachoma, a leading cause of preventable blindness, as a public health priority. These countries include Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, the Islamic Republic of Iran, the Lao People's Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu. The WHO's SAFE strategy, which stands for surgical repair, antibiotics, facial cleanliness, and environmental improvement, is being implemented in these countries with the support of the WHO and its partners. The SAFE strategy involves surgical repair to close corneal scars, the use of antibiotics to treat active trachoma, regular facial cleanliness, and environmental improvements, particularly focusing on improving access to clean water and sanitation to reduce transmission.

translations.source: Ngomzamo wesifo samehlo wamazwe angaphandle,antibhayothikhi azithromycin xhase ngePfizer ekuqedweni kwezinhlelo zokwenza zesu leSAFE. I-Benin isihlanganise isifo samehlo ukuqedwa nokungenelela kulabo abaenza okubhekene nezinye izifo ezijwayelekile ezinganakiwe (amaNTD) ngaphansi kwesambulela seNationa Programme for Communicable Disesases. Isifo samehlo ngesesithathu kuNTD ukuqedwa eBenin emva kwedracunculiasis (ngo-2009) negambiese yabantu eAfrika trypanosomiasis (ngo-2021). Imali isicwaninge umthelela wesifo samehlo nocwaningo lokubheka nokukhipha izingenelelo ukuthola okuqondwe ukuqedwa,ngaphandle kwezingqinamba zokuphepha ezifundeni zasenyakatho yezwe nezinkinga zepolitiki yezenhlalakahle eminyakeni yakamuva nje. Isifo samehlo ngesokuqala seNTD ukuqedwa eMali ngakho manje esijoyina iqoqo lomhlaba lamazwe angu-47 aseqede okungenani esisodwa seNTD.
translations.reference: Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD.
translations.hypothesis: In response to the global trachoma epidemic, the Nigerian government has launched a SAFE (Stop trachoma Elimination Programme) initiative in partnership with Pfizer. Benin is also working to eliminate trachoma through its National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third leading cause of preventable blindness in Benin, after onchocerciasis (river blindness) in 2009 and lymphatic filariasis (elephantiasis) in 2021. Funding is being allocated to trachoma elimination and research to improve the effectiveness of interventions, despite challenges such as security concerns in some areas and political instability in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD (neglected tropical disease) to be eliminated in Mali, and the country is now participating in the global effort to eliminate this disease.

translations.source: "Lokhu ukuzuza kwezempilo yomphakathi okujabulisayo," kusho uDkt Ibrahima Soce Fall uMqondisi woHlelo lweNTD eMhlabeni lweWHO. "I-Benin nemali ikhombisileukuthi kuqine kanjani isifiso sezepolitiki,ukuhlangana kwemikhakha eyahlukene,ukubheka nokukhuluma komphakathi kungasebenza kusivumelwano sokuthola ukuqedwa kwesio." Ukuqhubeka okubalulekile sekwenzekile ekulweni okubhekene nesifo samehlo eminyakeni embalwa edlule. Abantu abaningi badinga kwelashwa nge antibhayothikhi ukuqeda isifo samehlo eWHO Afrikan Region abafika ezigidini ezingu-84 kusuka kuzigidi ezingu-189 ngo-2014 kuya kuzigidi ezingu-105 kusuka ngoNhlangulana 2022. Inothi eliya kumhleli: Isifo samehlo (Trachoma) sizohlala siyinkinga yezempilo yomphakathi emazweni angu-41 (kusuka ngoNhlangulana 2022) ngesibalo esilinganiselwe kuzigidi ezingu-125 zabantu abaphila ezindaweni ezidinga ukungenelela okubhekene nalesifo.
translations.reference: “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease.
translations.hypothesis: "This is a remarkable achievement in public health," said Dr. Ibrahim Soce Fall, Director of the WHO Global Trachoma Programme. "The Benin and Mali governments have demonstrated how effective political commitment, collaboration between different sectors, and community engagement can be in achieving elimination of trachoma." Progress has also been made in addressing the issue of trachoma in recent years. The number of people requiring treatment with antibiotics for trachoma in the WHO African Region decreased from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million in 2022. Note to editor: Trachoma (a bacterial eye infection) remains a major public health problem in 41 countries (as of 2022), affecting an estimated 125 million people who need treatment.

translations.source: Isifo samehlo sitholakala kakhulu ezindaweni ezingamakhaya futhi ezihluphekayo e-Afrika, Maphakathi naseNingizimu neAmerica, Asia naseWestern Pacific kanye neMiddle East. I-WHO African Region itheleleke yisifo samehlo kubantu abayizigidi ezingu 105 abahlala ezindaweni eziyingcuphe,ezimele u-48% womthwalo wesifo samehlo emhlabeni. Isifo samehlo isona esihamba phambili ekuthelelaneni esidala ukungaboni emhlabeni wonke. Isifo sidalwa ukuthelelana ngegciwane iChlamydia trachomatis. Ukuthelelana kudluliseka kumuntu kuye kumuntu ngeminwe engcolile, izinambuzane, izimpukane esezihlangabezane nokungcola okuphuma emahlweni noma ikhala lomuntu othelelekile.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person.
translations.hypothesis: Trachoma, a form of blindness, is most prevalent in rural and underserved areas of Africa, as well as in the Americas, Asia, and the Western Pacific and Middle East. The World Health Organization's African Region estimates that over 105 million people are affected by trachoma, representing 48% of the global burden of the disease. Trachoma is the leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide. The infection is spread from person to person through direct or indirect contact with infected eyes or nasal discharge.

translations.source: Izinkinga zengcephe yendawo yokudluliselwa kwesifo samehlo okufaka ukungahlanzeki,imizi egcwele ukungatholi kahle amanzi nokusebenzisa izindawo zokuzikhulula ezifanele. Ngo-1996, iWHO yethula iWHO Alliane yokuqedwa kwesifo samehlo emhlabeni ngonyaka ka-2020 (GET2020). Nabanye ababambisani kwi-Alliance, iWHO yeseka ukwenza kwezwe kweSu lokuPhepha nokuqinisa amandla ezwe ngokhlolwa kwezobhubhane, ukubheka, ukuhlola, ukubhekisa iphrojekthi nokuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza ezizosiza ekuqedweni kwesifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi. Umgwaqo balazwe ogunyazwe nguMhlangano weZempilo eMhlabeni ngo-2020 ngesinqumo sawo 73(33) ibeka u-2030 njengosuku oluqondiwe olusha lokuqeda emhlabeni.
translations.reference: Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.
translations.hypothesis: Challenges in establishing a safe and effective water supply system for eye care facilities, including access to clean water and the use of appropriate sanitation facilities. In 1996, the WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Elimination of Blindness by 2020 (GET2020). Other partners in the Alliance, including the WHO, are working to achieve this goal through a comprehensive approach that includes surveillance, screening, treatment, and the integration of eye care services into existing health systems. The World Health Assembly resolution 73.33 adopted in 2020 sets 2030 as a new target date for the elimination of avoidable blindness globally.

translations.source: Izigidi ezingu-152 izingane ezizalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kushuminyaka elidlule Ezinganeni ezingu-10 ezizalwayo, eyodwa izalwa phambi kwesikhathi futhi emizuzwini engu-40 eyodwa kulezo ngane iyafa Amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kushuminyaka eledlule kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni Imithelela yengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu,ingcuphe yokunyuka kweCOVID-19 kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo Isu liyadingeka ngokushesha ukwenza ngcono ukuvikela ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okuhambisana nokunakekela okungcono ezinganeni ezikhahlamezekile kanye nemindeni yazo
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families
translations.hypothesis: Approximately 152 babies were stillborn in the previous year, compared to 10 babies born prematurely. This represents a significant increase in stillbirth rates, with one baby stillborn every 40 minutes. Factors contributing to this increase include conflict, changes in weather patterns, and the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic. There is an urgent need to improve the prevention of stillbirths, including better antenatal care for at-risk women and their families.

translations.source: Isilinganiso sezigindi ezingu-13.4 izingane ezizalwe ngaphambi kwesikhathi nokungafika esigidini esisodwa semithelela yokufa, ngokombiko omusha okhishiwe yi-United Nations ama-ejensi kanye nababambisani namhlanje. Lo mzuliswano wokulingana wengane eyodwa kweziyishumi ezisheshe zizalwe (ngaphambi kwamasonto angu-37 okukhulelwa) emhlabeni wonke. Ukusheshe uzalwe: ishuminyaka lokwenza ekuzalweni ngaohambi kwesikhathi, kukhiqizwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni (WHO) ne United Nations Children Fund (UNICEF) ndawonye nePMNCH -- ubudlelwane obukhulu emhlabeni babantu besifazane, izingane, nentsha, kuveza ukuuthusa bokuthula kokuphuthumayo kwezokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okunesikhathi eside kunganakiwe esikalini sakho sobucayi okuveza ukuqhubeka ekwenzeningcono ezempilo zezingane nokuphila. Umbiko ukubuyekezwa kulinganisa okuphuma kuWHO ne-UNICEF, izilungiselele neLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, ekusabalaleni kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Kukhona konke kutholakala ukuthi amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni kuleshuminyaka eledlule nezigidi ezingu-152 zezingane ezihluphekile ezisheshe zazalwa kusuka ngo-2010 kuya ku-2020.
translations.reference: An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.
translations.hypothesis: The rate of preterm births, at 13.4 per 1,000 live births, is significantly higher than the global average, according to a new report released today by the United Nations and its partners. This report highlights the alarming trend of preterm births in all regions of the world. Preterm birth: defined as birth before 37 weeks of gestation, the World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, and the Partnership for Maternal, Newborn, and Child Health (PMNCH) have jointly issued a call for urgent action to improve the care of preterm babies. The report emphasizes the need for better access to antenatal care, skilled birth attendants, and neonatal care for preterm infants. The findings show that preterm birth rates have remained relatively stable across all regions in the past decade, with an estimated 152 million preterm babies born worldwide between 2010 and 2020.

translations.source: Ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi manje sekuyimbangela eholayo ekufeni kwezingane,ukubala okungaphezu kuka 1 wokungu 5 wakho konke ukufa okwenzeka ngaphambi kosuku lokuzalwa lweminyaka emihlanu. Abaphilayo kwabazalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi babhekana nemiphumela yezempilo impilo yabo yonke, ngokukhula kokujwayeleka kokukhubazeka kanye nokubambezeleka kokukhula. Izikhala zokuphila yisifunda, imali engenayo, ibala Ukwakhela emmbikweni wesikhonkwane esihlokweni sango-2012,lokhu kusheshe uzalwe, ishuminyaka, umbiko unikeza ukubhekwa okukhulu kokusabalala kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nomthelela oqinile kwabesifazane, imiphakathi nezomnotho. Kujwayelekile lapho izingane zizalelwe khona kuzonquma ukuthi zizophila yini.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive.
translations.hypothesis: Being born prematurely is a serious condition that can have long-term health consequences for infants. It is estimated that more than 1 in 5 babies are born prematurely. Premature babies are at increased risk of developing a number of health problems, including respiratory distress, feeding difficulties, and developmental delays. These problems can have a significant impact on the baby's quality of life. Premature babies are also more likely to experience social and economic challenges, as they may require more intensive care and support from their families.

translations.source: Umbiko uqaphela ukuthi uyedwa kuphela kwabangu-10 abantwana abasheshe bazalwa (<amasonto angu-28) abaphilayo emzaweni anemali encane engenayo,uma uqhathanisa kwangaphezu kwangu-9 ku-10 emazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Izikhala zokungalingani ezihlobene nebala, nobuzwe nemali engenayo nokthola ukunakekelwa okufanele kukhomba ukuphila kokusheshe uzalwe ukufa kanye nokukhubazeka ngisho nasemazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Southern Asia nesub-Saharan Afrika banamazinga aphezulu okusheshe kuzalwe kulezizizfunda futhi abhekene nengcuphe yokushona kwezingane ephezulu. Ndawonye, lezi zifunda ezimbili zibala ngaphezu kuka-65% wezingane ezisheshe zazalwa emhlabeni. Umbiko uphinde uveze umthelela wengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu nokulimala kwendawo, COVID-19 nokunyuka kokubiza kwempilo okunyusa ingcuphe kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo.
translations.reference: The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.
translations.hypothesis: The report highlights that preterm births are a significant problem, affecting only 1 in 10 babies born before 28 weeks of gestation, compared to over 9 in 10 babies in wealthier countries. The disparities in preterm birth rates are linked to factors such as poverty, lack of access to healthcare, and inadequate prenatal care, which disproportionately affect low-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth in the world, and these regions also have the highest rates of infant mortality. Overall, these two regions account for over 65% of all preterm births worldwide. The report also notes the impact of conflict, climate change, and natural disasters on preterm birth rates, as well as the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and its associated increase in maternal and infant mortality.

translations.source: Isibonelo, ukungcola komoya kulinganiselwa ukuthi kunomthelela wezigidi ezingu-6 zokusheshe kuzalwe unyaka ngamunye. Kungafika ku 1 kwabangu 10 izingane ezisheshe zazalwa ezizalwe emazweni abuthaka angu-10 akhahlamezwe izinkinga zabantu ngokokuhlaziya okusha embikweni. Ingcuphe yomama abakhulelwe, njengokukhulelwa kwamatshitshi kusondelene nokusheshe uzale. Lokhu kugqamisa isidingo sokuqinisekisa ukuthola ezempilo zokuzala nezocansi, okufaka ukusebenza kokuhlela umndeni, ukunakekelwa okusezingeni eliphezulu kwabakhulelwe nangesikhathi sokuzala. Ajenda yokwenza: utshalomali oluningi ezweni nokushoshova okuholwa ngabazali
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism
translations.hypothesis: For example, the impact of air pollution is estimated to cause six million premature deaths each year. This figure is based on the fact that air pollution is a major risk factor for a number of diseases, including heart disease, stroke, and lung cancer. Factors that contribute to air pollution, such as traffic congestion and industrial emissions, can also have a negative impact on reproductive health. This highlights the need for effective policies to reduce air pollution, including measures to promote sustainable transportation and reduce industrial emissions. One possible solution is to invest in public transportation and encourage the use of bicycles and other forms of active transport.

translations.source: Ishuminyaka eledlulesibone ukukhula kwezishoshovu emphakathini ekuvinjweni kokusheshe kuzalwe kwezingane, okuholwa uhlelokuxhumana lwabazali, izifundiswa zempilo, izifundiswa umphakathi nabanye. Ezweni lonke amaqoqo emindeni ekhahlamezekile ngokusheshe kuzalwe kwengane alokhu ephambili ekumeleni ukuthola ukunakekelwa okungcono nokushinthwa kwenqubomgomo nokweseka imindeni. Okuholela kuInternational Maternal Newborn Helath Conference, eKapa eNingizimu Afrika kusuka ngo-8-11 kuNhlaba, iWHO, i-UNICEF, i-UNFPA nePMNCH bamemezela ukulandela izenzo ukwenza ngcono ukunakekela kwabesifazane nabazelwe ubukhulu bezingcuphe okuvela kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Ukunyuswa kotshalomali:ukuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza zamazwe angaphandle nezendawo ukusiza ezempilo zomama nezingane ezizelwe, ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okuphezulu kuyanikezwa uma noma nalapho kudingeka khona. Ukunyuswa kokwenza: Ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe ngamazwe kokuqhubeka ngokwenza okusungulwe yinqubomgomo yezwe kokunakekelwa komama nezingane ezizelwe.
translations.reference: The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families.
