We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
translations.hypothesis: Member states of the WHO, along with their partners, are working together to promote and advance efforts to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for health emergencies and pandemics. We recognize the crucial role of the community and all sectors in coordinating and ensuring effective responses during health crises. We draw on lessons from past outbreaks and pandemics, particularly those caused by respiratory viruses. These viruses continue to pose a significant threat to global health, causing widespread illness, death, and disruption to essential services. They also undermine economic stability, exacerbate inequalities, and hinder access to life-saving tools and resources for all. This underscores the need for continued investment in pandemic preparedness and response.

translations.source: Ukusebenza kokuzilungiselela kuncike kubukhulu bokuhlela nokwenza kokuxhumana. Ukuzimisela kweqoqo kuzenze ezilandelayo kuzobonisa ukuqhubeka okutholakele ngoZibandlela 2025 futhi kuzochazwa (Ukuzilungiselela nokubekezela kokusabisayo okuvelayo (PRET) ukubheka umhlahlandlela: Ukulungiselelwa kokubuyekeziwe ukuhlela okuqinisekisa izenzo zokuhamba phambili nokucabanga izifundo zezigemegeme ezedlule. Ukubona ingcuphe efakwe amagciwane ezokuphefumula, ukuhlela kobhubhane lamagciwane ezokuphefumula ancike kuzingqikithi ezihlonzwe kusifundo sePRET #1: Ukuhlelela ubhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula kuhamba phambili
translations.reference: Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority
translations.hypothesis: The level of preparation depends on the scope and complexity of the project. The success of the project will be demonstrated in the following phases, which will build upon the foundation laid in Phase 1. This includes a revised plan that ensures ongoing progress and addresses any challenges encountered in previous phases. This plan incorporates the use of ventilation systems, and the design of these systems depends on the specific requirements of the project #1: Designing ventilation systems

translations.source: Ukunyusa ukuxhumana kubabambisani ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ukuhlela ngezinhlelo zokuxhumana nokusebenzisana. Lokhu kufaka ukwakha izinhlelo ezilinganayo ukwenza imisebenzi ngokuhlanganyela; Nokwabelana ngolwazi ekwenzeni okuhle, izingqinamba kanye namathuba. Ukunikela ngotshalomali olugcinekile, izimali nokubheka kokulungela ubhubhane ngokugxila ekubhekeni izikhala ezihlonze ngesikhathi sobhubhane olwedlule. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 luphendule ukukhombisa ukuthi yikuphi okungazuzwa ngokuzibophezela kwezepolitiki, ukukhuluma komphakathi kanye noxhasomali.
translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding.
translations.hypothesis: How can we improve communication and collaboration in disaster preparedness and response? This includes developing clear communication systems for coordinating efforts; sharing information about risks, challenges, and opportunities; and providing financial resources and technical expertise to support disaster preparedness. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the importance of effective communication and collaboration in responding to disasters.

translations.source: Phakathi nalomsebenzi ukuqinisekisa ukulingana ukulungela obhubhane olulandelayo ndawonye. Ukwenza komphakathi wonke kuyadingeka ukwenza inqubekela phambili evezwe kule simemezelo sokwenza. Ukwenza kumele kuqinise imiphakathi ebekezelayo; egcinayo nokugcineka nokwakha izindlela zezinhlelo; nokubambisa kwamandla amakhulu kokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula. Lesi simemezelo sokwenza sakhishwa eKuzilungiseleni nokubekezela kokuvela kokusabisayo (PRET): Umhlangano womhlaba wekusasa lobhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula obanjwe ngo-24-26 Mbasa 2023 eGeneva, eSwitzerland.
translations.reference: At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis: In addition to this work, ensuring compatibility for the next phase of development is also crucial. Creating a collaborative environment is essential for the success of this initiative. This should involve fostering open communication; promoting knowledge sharing; and building strong partnerships for rapid response and adaptation. This initiative is being implemented within the framework of the Global Preparedness Initiative for Pandemic Influenza (PREP): a meeting of the World Health Organization held on 24-26 May 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.

translations.source: I-WHO inxenxa ukunyuswa kokwenza ngamathuluzi aphakanyisiwe ukulwa nomalaleveva Ukumaka uSuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni, Inhlangano Yezempilo Yomhlaba (WHO) izokhipha isimememzelo sokunyusa ukwenza kokungenelela okukhona nokusha ukusindisa izimpilo kumalaleveva. Okungafika kuzigidi ezingu-1.5 izingane ezisengcupheni enkulu yokugula nokufa okusuka kumalaleveva eGhana, eKenya naseMalawi manje sebethole umgomo wokuqala kamalaleveva, RTS, S/AS01 (RTS,S), siyabonga kuhlelo lokuhlola oluqhubekayo oluxhunyaniswa yiWHO. Ukuhlolwa komgomo wamalaleveva, owethulwe ngo-2019,kwenyusa ukulingana kokuthola ukuvikelwa kumalaleveva kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu futhi kusindisa izimpilo. Ukwenziwe kakhulu, iWHO ilinganisela ukuthi umgomo kamalaleveva ungasingisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane ngonyaka.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO is investigating the use of innovative tools to combat malaria. The World Health Organization (WHO) will launch a campaign to promote the use of existing and new tools to save lives from malaria. This could prevent millions of children from dying from malaria in countries like Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, which are currently experiencing a major malaria outbreak. The RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S) vaccine, developed in collaboration with the WHO, is one of the promising malaria vaccines currently available. The malaria vaccine, which was first introduced in 2019, has shown to significantly improve protection against malaria in young children. The WHO estimates that the vaccine could save tens of thousands of children's lives each year.

translations.source: "Sinamathuluzi ukwehlisa umalaleveva,iphekheji yokungenenela ebandakanya ukulawula ivektha,imithi yokuvikela, ukuhlola nokwelapha," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Lokhu kulandelwa ngumgomo ophephile nosebenzayo kamalaleveva, ongasindisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane unyaka ngamunye. Ngotshalomali olugcinekile nokunyuka kwemizamo ukufika kulabo abasengcupheni kakhulu, ukuqedwa kwamalaleveva emazweni amaningi sekuyafika. "Usuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni 2023 lumakwe ngaphansi kwengqikithi, "Isikhathi sokuwqeda umalaleveva, tshala, yenza kabusha yenza". Kule ngqikithi, iWHO inxenxa okunye ukwenza okusebenzayo kwamathuluzi akhona namasu okuvikela, ukuxilonga nokwelapha umalaleveva, ikakhulukazi kulesosibalo esingcindezelekile.
translations.reference: “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.
translations.hypothesis: "We have the tools to prevent, treat, and manage malaria, including rapid diagnostic tests, effective antimalarial drugs, insecticide-treated bed nets, and vaccines," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. "This has led to a significant reduction in malaria cases and deaths, saving millions of children's lives each year. However, challenges remain in ensuring that everyone has access to these life-saving interventions, and in overcoming the remaining barriers to malaria elimination in many countries. World Malaria Day 2023 is being observed under the theme, "Invest in Zero Malaria: Our Entire World is at Stake."

translations.source: Ngokombiko wokugcina wamalaleveva emhlabeni,oshicilelwe ngoZibandlela 2022, bekunesilinganiso sika-247 wezigidi zamacala amasha kamalaleveva ngo-2021. I-WHO African Region iyaqhubeka ukuthwala umthwalo onzima wesifo- okuballelwa esilinganiswenisa ku-95% wamacala kamalaleveva (u-234 wezigidi) no-96% wokufa (593 000) ngo-2021. Kungafika ku-80% ukufa okudalwa umalaleveva eSifundeni se-Afrika obukukubantwana abangaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Amasu amasha namathuluzi Amazweasenze inqubekela phambili ekwandiseni ukungena kumisebenzi yamalaleveva kusibalo sabantu esisengcupheni kakhulu.
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations.
translations.hypothesis: According to the latest global report on malaria, published in 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to bear the heaviest burden of the disease, with 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of malaria deaths (593,000) occurring in Africa in 2021. Malaria is a leading cause of death among children under five in the African Region. However, new tools and strategies are being implemented to increase access to malaria prevention and treatment, leading to a significant reduction in the number of cases and deaths.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kokuqhubeka abantu abaningi abasengcupheni yamalaleveva basantula ukungena emisebenzini engavimba, noma itthole bese yelapha isifo. Izingqinamba ekwandeni kokungena kumisebenzi kamalaleveva sezihlanganisisiwe,ikakhulukazi esu-Saharan Afrika, ngobhubhane lweCOVID-19, ukujika izinkinga zobuntu, uxhasomali olunezivimbo, izinhlelo zokubheka ezintekenteke, ukwehla ekwenzeni kwamathuluzi okulwa ngqo nomalaleveva. Ukubhekana nalokhu okusabisayo nokweseka amazwe ekwakheni izinhlelo zokubekezela zamalaleveva, iWHO isanda kushicilela amasu amasha nomhlahlandlela: isu elisha lelinesidakamizwa esingenamalaleveva ukuvika eAfrika; umzamo omusha wokumisa ukusabalala kweAnophelesi stephensi ezindaweni zasemadolobheni;
translations.reference: Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;
translations.hypothesis:
Despite the ongoing efforts of many organizations to prevent and treat malaria, access to effective interventions remains a significant challenge, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa. The COVID-19 pandemic, coupled with existing challenges such as poverty, conflict, and inadequate health systems, has further exacerbated the situation. The availability of effective malaria control tools is also limited, particularly in remote areas.

In response to this urgent need, the WHO has recently released new guidelines and recommendations: a new insecticide-based malaria treatment for use in Africa; a new strategy for controlling the Anopheles stephensi mosquito in urban areas;

translations.source: umhlahlandlela omusha, uthuthukiswe ndawonye yiWHO neUN-Habitat, ukuyalela babaholi bedolobha ekulawulweni kwemalalaeveva edolobheni. ithuluzi elisha ukusiza amazwe ahlola umalaleveva wabio wezinhlelo zokubheka nokuhlonza izindawo zotshalomali I-WHO iphinde yanyusa ukwethembeka, ukubalula nokuthola iziphakamiso zemalaleveva. Ukuhlanganiswa kwemiyalelo yeWHO kwamalaleveva sekukhona manje ngezinkundla ezimbili zedijithali: iMAGICapp kanye ne-"MalariaToolkit" isisetshenziswa. I-WHO igqugquzela amazwe ukuthi ahlanganise kuziphakamiso ekuhlelweni kwezifo zendawo kumthelela omkhulu.
translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.
translations.hypothesis: The new guidelines, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, provide city leaders with guidance on malaria prevention and control within urban areas. The new tool helps countries to assess the risk of malaria transmission and to develop effective control strategies. WHO has also strengthened its capacity to provide technical support and guidance on malaria. The new guidelines are available online through two digital platforms: the MAGICapp and the "Malaria Toolkit". WHO encourages countries to integrate these guidelines into their national malaria control programs.

translations.source: Izindlela zokungenelela okusha Ukuqhubeka kotshalomali ekuthuthukisweni nokubekwa kwemigomo emisha kamalaleveva namathuluzi esizukulwane esilandelayo kuzobalulekaukuthola okuqondwe ngomalaleveva emhlabeni 2030. Umgomo wesibili kamalaleveva, umgomo iR21/Matrix-M (R21), uma ugunyaziwe ungasiza ukuvala isikhala esikalekayo phakathi kwesaplayi mokufuneka nokuqhubeka ukwehlisa ukugula kwezingane nokufa okudalwa umalaleveva. Kuhamba phambili kuWHO ukuqhubeka ngokubheka kangoti kweR21 malaleveva ngesikhathi sekwengezwe okuphepha okubalulekile,nemininingo kusuka ekuqhubekeni kukaR21 isigaba 3 sesikhona nokinikezelwa kuWHO. Isigaba sokukhiqiza iRTS,S umgomo wamalaleveva emazweni engeziwe eAfrika kulindeleke ukuthi aqale ngokuqala kuka-2024.
translations.reference: Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.
translations.hypothesis: New approaches to vaccination: Continued efforts to achieve universal vaccination coverage and introduce new vaccines and tools for the next generation will be crucial in achieving the goal of zero measles deaths globally by 2030. The second measles vaccine, R21/Matrix-M (R21), has shown promise in preventing measles in clinical trials and is expected to be a valuable tool in reducing measles morbidity and mortality. The World Health Organization (WHO) is continuing to evaluate the safety and efficacy of R21, and is considering recommending its use in routine immunization programs. The RTS,S vaccine, a single-dose measles vaccine for children in endemic countries, is expected to be introduced in several African countries starting in 2024.

translations.source: Endaweni yokulawula ivekitha,kukhona imikhiqizoemisha engu-28 kokuzayo kweR&D. Amathuluzi asabhekisiswa afka, isibonelo, izinhlobo ezintsha zamanethi okulapha izinambuzane, izicuphomziqonde ukudonsa omiyane, isipeshali ripheleli, okwasendlini, (i-eaves tubes) nobunjiniyela bofuzo lwemiyane. Abacwaningi bahambisa phambili ukuthuthuka kokulashwa okungeyona i-ACTS emikhakheni wemithi omele umalaleveva ngenxa yokuphuthuma nokusabalala kokungavuminokuncane kwe-artemisinin. Ukdevelophwa kwemithi yesizukulwane esilandelayo, njenge ACTS, okusebenzisa ukuhlanganiswa kwe-artemisinin nezidakamizwa zimbili, kusendleleni kuzokwehlisa ingcuphe yokungavumi kwesidakamizwa. Amanothi aya kumhleli: ngolwazi olwengeziwe ekukhankaseni ngoSuku lukamalaleveva eMhlabeni lweWHO, vakashela u-http://www.who.int/campains/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis: In the area of drug development, there are 28 new R&D products in the pipeline. These include novel antimalarial drugs, such as new generations of artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs), novel drug delivery systems, and new formulations for artemisinin. Researchers are also working to develop new strategies for combating malaria, including the use of artemisinin in combination with other drugs. For more information on World Malaria Day, please visit the WHO website at http://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023

translations.source: Uhlelo loKudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe i-mRNA iya esigabeni esilandelayo ekuthuthukeni kwayo Ukwethulwa kwehabhu yezobuchwepheshe imRNA indawo e-Afrigen, eNingizimu Afrika. Ababambisani bahlangane eKapa ukuxoxa ngesigaba esilandelayo semRNA umgomo ngokuthuthukisa nokwabelana ngolwazi. Ngaphezu kwababambiqhaza abangu-200 bamazwe angaphandle abasebenza ngoHlelo loKudlulisela lezoBuchwepheshe beMRNA abahlangane eKapa kulelisonto ukuze bahlangane bebonana. Ukujoyinwa iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) uMqondisi Jikelele uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus noDkt Joe Phaahla uNgqongqoshe weZempilo noNgqongqoshe Wezimboni nokuhweba uMnumzane Ebrahim waseNingizimu Afrika ndawonye nezikhulu ezisezingeni eliphezulu eziphuma emazweni axhasile, lomhlangano owehlukile ubheke ukuqhubeka kusukela ukwethula kweWHO neMedicines Patent Pool (MPP) ngoNhlangulana 2021.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021.
translations.hypothesis: The mRNA Transfer Program is moving into its next phase of development. The launch of the mRNA Transfer Program in Africa, specifically in South Africa, has been met with enthusiasm. Participants will gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the program, focusing on promoting and sharing knowledge. Over 200 international participants from outside South Africa have gathered in Cape Town this week to attend the program. The World Health Organization (WHO) Director-General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, and South African Minister of Health and Trade, Dr. Joe Phaahla, along with other high-level officials from developing countries, will also be present at the event. The event will focus on the ongoing collaboration between the WHO and the Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) since their launch in 2021.

translations.source: "Ngiyajabula ukuba lapha eKapa kanye nababambisani bethu ukuzokweseka imodeli egcinekayo yokudluliselwa kobuchwepheshe mRNA ukunikeza amazwe anemali engenayo encane kuya kwephakathi nendawo ukuthola ngokulingana umgomo kanye neminye imikhiqizo yezempilo esindisa izimpilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngijabule kakhulu ngokuzuziwe kubona bonke ababambe iqhaza kulephrojekthi,ngaphansi kweminyaka emibili sesikhombisile ukuthi uma sisebenza ngokubambisana, siphumelela sonke." Ababambiqhaza bomhlangano kufaka ababambisani bomkhiqizo wasemazweni angu-15 oHlelweni, ongoti abaphambili, imboni abameli bomphakathi kanye nabaxhasimali. Ngesikhathi somhlangano wezinsuku esinhlanu, ababambiqhaza bazokwabelana ngokuqhubeka baxoxe ngokubucayi kwezinto zokugcineka koHlelo njengezinkinga zegunyabunikazi nezindlela zokulawula kanyenesayensi yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA nokusebenza okubalulekile okuhlobene neLMIC kwezinye izndawo zezifo njengeHIV nesifo sofuba. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 sekukale ngaphansi ukuthi ukungalingani okuphelele kukhona ekutholeni imikhiqizo yezempilo, ikakhulukazi imigomo.
translations.reference: “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.
translations.hypothesis: "I am delighted to be here with our partners to launch a groundbreaking mRNA vaccine delivery model that will provide low- and middle-income countries with equitable access to this life-saving technology," said the Director-General of WHO, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "I am particularly excited to see the collaboration between all the stakeholders involved in this project, and I am confident that, working together, we will achieve our common goals." The participants in the five-day meeting include representatives from the 15-country Global Fund, leading experts in vaccine development, civil society organizations, and donors. During the meeting, participants will discuss the ongoing efforts to implement the Global Fund's mRNA vaccine program, including challenges related to manufacturing, distribution, and the use of mRNA technology in other areas of global health, such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the urgent need for equitable access to essential medicines, particularly vaccines.

translations.source: Kusukela ngoNdasa 2023, ngaphezu kweminyaka emithathu ngemva kokuthi Iwho imemezele iCOVID-19 njengePublic Health of International Concern (PHEIC), u-69.7% wesibalo sabantu emhlabeni sebethole okungenani idosi eyodwa yomgomo weCOVID-19. Okuqaphelekayo lengxenye izohlala ingaphansi kuka-30% emazweni anemali engenayo encane (amaLIC). Loli Hlelo luhlose ukuxhasa ekutholeno okulinganayo emigomweni ye-mRNA ngokunyusa ukuhambisa kwamandla okukhiqiza okugcinekayo kuwona wonke amaLMIC, ukwandisa okwesifunda nokubambisana kwaphakathi nezifunda nokudevelopha nokusiza ukusebenza kwasendaweni ngokwenziwa kokuqeqeshwa nokwesekwa ngongoti. Yikuphi okwehlukile kumodeli yoKudluliselwa kweZobuchwepheshe kwe-mRNA kuhlelo olunezinto eziningi oluvumela ukwabelana ngobuchwepheshe,ukuthola okuningi ukuze ukukhiqizwa ngokwesifunda nendawo kulabo abadingayo kungafinyelela ngokuphindiwe. UDkt Phaahla, uNgqongqoshe weZempilo eNingizimu Afrika, uthe: "Esikubona namhlanje lapha,umzumzu womlando, uHlelo oluhlose ukusiza iLMIC ngohlelokuxhumana lokubambisana emhlabeni.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network.
translations.hypothesis: Since the World Health Organization declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) in March 2020, 69.7% of the global population has received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. However, this coverage is significantly lower in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). This program aims to support equitable access to COVID-19 vaccines by increasing the capacity of LMICs to produce and distribute safe and effective vaccines, expanding vaccine manufacturing and distribution networks, and providing training and support to local healthcare providers. One of the key features of this multi-faceted mRNA vaccine rollout program is its focus on sharing expertise and resources, enabling LMICs to develop their own vaccine manufacturing capabilities and ensure that they have access to the vaccines they need. Dr. Phaahla, Minister of Health in South Africa, said: "What we are seeing today is a historic opportunity to use a global partnership to help LMICs achieve equitable access to COVID-19 vaccines.

translations.source: Ngiyajabula ukubona inqubekela phambili eyenziwe esikhathini esifishane kangaka, siyakwamukela ukwesekwa okusuka emazweni amaningi ahlukene-amazwe afana neNingizimu Afrika anokukhiqizwa okuqinile anamandla, futhi sizimisele ukusebenza ndawonye, ukufunda nokwabelana kuthina sonke." Ungqongqoshe Blade Nzimande weMfundo ePhakeme neSayensi, uthe: "Umbono wethu weHabhu yoKudlulisela ubuchwepheshe be-mRNA kungaphezu kweCOVID-19 nje. Amandla okuthi siyakha afuna usizo kuthina ukubhekana nobhubhane lwesikhathi esizayo imigomo yalo engasebenzisa inkundla efanayo yobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Kungumbono osubeke amehlo awo ekubhekaneni nezifo ezivamile ezindaweni zethu njengoMalaleveva, isifo sofuba, iHIV ne-AIDS." Lomcimbi uphinde umake uhambo olubalulekile loHlelo nokwethulwa kwendawo yeHabhu yobuchwepheshe ne-mRNA e-Afrigen eKapa kukhona uDkt Tedros noDkt Matshidiso Moeti weWHO Regional Director for Afrika, Charles Gore uMqondisi oMkhulu weMPP kanye noNgqongqoshe abahlonishwayo ababamba iqhaza ekusikweni kweribhini emcimbini e-Afrigen.
translations.reference: I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis:
I am delighted to see the progress made in such a short time, and I welcome the support from many different countries, including those in Southern Africa, which have the resources and expertise to make a real difference. We are committed to working together, learning from each other, and sharing our knowledge and experiences.

Education Minister Blade Nzimande said: "Our vision for the mRNA vaccine hub is not just about COVID-19. It is about building a platform that can be used to develop vaccines for other diseases, such as malaria, tuberculosis, HIV, and AIDS."

The event also marked an important milestone in the development of the mRNA vaccine hub at Afrigen in Cape Town, with the participation of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, the WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore, the Director General of MPP, and other key stakeholders.

translations.source: USolwazi Petro Terblance, uMqondisi oMkhulu we-Afrigen, uthe: "Iqembu lonke le-Afrigen liyajabula ukufika kulokhu okubalulekile nokuqedwa kwenkundla yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Lenkundla inendawo ekupheleni kokuthuthukiswa komgomo mRNA nokukhiqizwa kwendawo lapho ihabhu ye-mRNA yomgomo weCOVID-19 ikhandidethi AfriVac 2121 njengamanje isezingeni lesikalo esiphezulu. Ngaphezu kwezinyanga ezingu-18 Afrigen isihambe uguquko olukhulu ngokwesekwa kohlelokuxhumana lababambisani nabeluleki abenza loluHlelo. Sesikhulise amandla ethu nokukwazi ukuhlangabezana nezinga elihle eliphezulu lokuthuthukiswa komgomo we-mRNA, ukusiza injongo yokwakha ukukwazi okugcinekile kumaLMIC ukukhiqiza imigomo ye-mRNA.". I-Afrigen isisungulwe ngempumelelo uhlelo lokukhiqizwa kwemigomo yeCOVID-19 ngesikalo selebhu njengamanje kuyanyuka uhlelo ngezinga elifanele lokukhiqiza ngezigaba imigomo ukuthi isetshenziswe ngesigaba sesibili noma sokuqala sokuhlola kwezokwelapha kumazinga eGMP.
translations.reference: Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards.
translations.hypothesis: Mr. Petro Terblanche, the Senior Director of Afrigen, stated: "The entire Afrigen team is delighted to be a part of this important milestone and the completion of the mRNA manufacturing facility. This facility represents a significant step forward in the development of mRNA technology and provides a platform for the production of mRNA vaccines, including the current Afrigen 2121 vaccine, which is currently at a high level of efficacy. Over the past 18 months, Afrigen has made significant progress in establishing a robust and scalable mRNA manufacturing platform, enabling the company to contribute to the global effort to ensure that LMICs (low- and middle-income countries) have access to high-quality mRNA vaccines." Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing platform that is currently producing vaccines at a high level of quality and scale, enabling the company to supply vaccines to both the first and second phases of clinical trials.

translations.source: Kuhlelo oluhambisanayo, i-Afrigen izoqhubeka nokwenza ukuqeqesha nokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe kubabambisani bohlelokuxhumana. Abaxhasi badlala indima ebalulekile ekwesekeni uHlelo ngesamba soxhaso kuze kube yimanje ngezigidi ezingu-117 neFrance ekubeni ngeyokuqala ukuxhasa umsebenzi wokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Ababekhona emhlanganweni bekungabamele abaphuma e-European Commission, iBelgium, iGermany kanye neNorway, iCanada, i-Africa Union, iNingizimu Afrika kanye ne-ELMA Foundation. UCaroline Delany, uMqondisi Jikelele weGlobal Affairs Canada waseSouthern and Eastern Afrika, uthe: "iCanada iqinisekisa ukuqgubeka nokweseka kwe-mRNA Technology Transfer Programme futhi siyajabula ukumemezela ukuqhubeka kokunikelwa kwezigidi ezingu-15 ngamadola aseCanada emhlanganweni namhlanje. Lokhu kuletha iminikelo eyisamba yeCanada kuHlelo lwezigidi ezingu-45 kumadola aseCanada.
translations.reference: In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.
translations.hypothesis: In a collaborative effort, Afrigen will continue to provide training and expertise in vaccine development and manufacturing to its partner organizations. The partnership represents a significant contribution to the program, with Afrigen providing over $117 million in funding and France providing the first mRNA vaccine manufacturing support. The meeting was attended by representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, Norway, Canada, the African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, Deputy Director General of Global Affairs Canada for Southern and Eastern Africa, stated: "Canada is committed to the success of the mRNA Technology Transfer Program and we are pleased to announce the continued provision of $15 million in Canadian funding to the program today. This brings Canada's total contribution to the program to $45 million.

translations.source: Lokhu kwesekwa koxhasomali ekusebenzeni kweHabhu eNingizimu Afrika kanye nababambisani bohlelokuxhumana emhlabeni wonke. Siyakhulwa kakhulu ekubalulekeni kokwakha ukukwazi ezingeni lendawo nelesifunda". UMnu Martin Seychell, uSekela Mqondisi omkhulu we-European Commission esho. "I-EC isivele ixhase ngezigidi ezingu-€40 ukusungula ihabhu yokudlulisela yezobuchwepheshe kweMRNA futhi isanda kusayina esinye isibonelelo nge-EIB kwezigidi ezingu-€15.5 ukusiza ukwandisa amandla okukhiqizwa kwemigomo. Lokhu yingxenye yotshalomali lonke ngaphansi kwesu le EU Global Gateway, lapho ngaphezu kwesigidigidi esisodwa sama-euro selihlanganisiwe ngaphansi komzamo weTeam Europe ekukhiqizweni kwemikhiqizo yezempilo. Manje sekubalulekile ukuphinde sibhekane nokulawula okungenzeka nokukhona okufunakalyo, ukuqinisekisa ukuthiakukhona ukukhiqiza kuphela kwemigomo esezingeni eliphezulu kuzona zonke izifunda emhlabeni kodwa nokuphindeka kokutholakala okulinganayo kwaleyo migomo."
translations.reference: These funds support the running of the Hub in South Africa as well as network partners around the world. We firmly believe in the importance of building capacity at local and regional level”. Mr Martin Seychell, Deputy Executive Director European Commission, said. “The EC has already contributed €40 million to the establishment of the mRNA technology transfer Hub and has recently signed another grant with the EIB for €15,5 million to facilitate the expansion of vaccines manufacturing capacity. This is part of the overall investment under the EU Global Gateway strategy, where more than €1 Billion have been mobilised under the Team Europe Initiative on Manufacturing health products. It is now particularly important to also address possible regulatory and demand bottlenecks, to ensure not only production of vaccines of the highest standard in all regions of the world, but also rapid and equitable access to those vaccines.”
translations.hypothesis: This initiative supports the work of the Global Vaccine Alliance and its partners in providing vaccines to people in Africa and around the world. It is particularly focused on building capacity at the local and regional level. "The European Commission is committed to supporting this important work," said Mr. Martin Seychell, Head of Global Health at the European Commission. "The EC has already committed €40 million to establish a global mRNA vaccine distribution hub, and has just signed another agreement with the EIB for €15.5 million to support increased vaccine production. This is part of our broader commitment under the EU Global Gateway, which aims to mobilize over €1 billion in investment in health products and services. It is now crucial to address the potential for vaccine nationalism and ensure that vaccines are distributed equitably across all regions of the world, while also ensuring that sufficient supplies are available for all countries."

translations.source: Ukuqokwa kobuholi oubalulekile okwenziwelwe ukuhambisa indlela yesu nemizamo yeWHO Inhlangano yeZempilo yoMhlaba (WHO) sebeqoke izikhulu eziphezulu iminyaka emihlanu ukuba ngabaholi eqembini labaholi ekomkhulu eGeneva. Ukuqokwa okusha kulandela ukuphinde kuqokwe uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus kuhlandla lesibili leminyaka emihlanu njengoMqondisi Jikelele. Ubuholi bonkebeqembu buhlanganiswe ukuhambisana nokuhamba phambili kweNhlangano kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo nokusebenzisana noMqondisi Jikelele ukuhambisa okuya kukhona okuhamba phambili ne-ajenda yentshisekelo yoguquko lweWHO. Ukuqhuba inqubekela phambili yokwenza kweWHO 13th General Programme of WORK (GPW13) ukuthola okuqondiwe "okungezigidigidi ezintathu" nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSuatainable Development Goals, umsebenzi weWHO kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo egxile kokuhamba phambili okuhlanu:
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities:
translations.hypothesis: The re-election of key WHO leaders for a second five-year term has been confirmed, with Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus being re-elected as Director-General. The new term will see the leadership team work together to advance the WHO's agenda for the next five years, in collaboration with the Director-General. The re-election comes after the WHO successfully completed its 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), which aimed to achieve "three billion" health-related Sustainable Development Goals. The WHO's focus for the next five years will be on:

translations.source: ukwenza ukulandelana ngendlela okuya ekugqugquzeleni ezempilo nokuvikela isifo ngokubhekana nomsuka ozidalayo; ushintsho olwenzekayo kuzinhlelo zezempilo okuya ekunakekelweni kwezempilo zokuqala; ukuqinisa izinhlelo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula; amahhanisi amandla esayensi,ukwenza kabusha,idatha nezobuchwepheshe ukwenzangcono ezempilo; kanye nokuqinisa iWHO njengabanegunya lokuhola kwezempilo zomhlaba nokuthola umthelela emazweni.
translations.reference: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries.
translations.hypothesis: promoting healthy lifestyles and disease prevention through evidence-based interventions; transforming public health programs to focus on primary healthcare; strengthening emergency preparedness and response systems; leveraging scientific expertise, innovation, and data to improve health outcomes; and enhancing the role of WHO as a global leader in health and advocating for health in countries.

translations.source: Qembu labaholi ekomkhulu, linenamba elinganayo kubantu besifazane nabesilisa, bazosebenza ngokuhlanganyela nesifunda seWHO namahhovisi emazweni. Amalungu amasha eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu:uDkt Jeremy Farrar ozoba nguSosayensi omkhulu weWHO kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Ukuqokwa kukaDkt Farrar kwamenyezelwa ngoZibandlela 2022. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Farrar uzobheka umkhakha wezesayensi, ukuhlanganisa ndawonye ngoti abaphambili nohlelokuxhumana kwezesayensi nokwenza kabusha emhlabeni wonke ukuyalela, ukudevelopha nokuhambisa ezempilo ezisezingeni eliphezulu inqubomgomo nemisebenzi kubantu abayidinga kakhulu. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO uDkt Farrar ubenguMqondisi weWellcome Trust.
translations.reference: The headquarters leadership team, comprising equal numbers of women and men, will work jointly with WHO regional and country offices. New members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Jeremy Farrar will become WHO’s Chief Scientist as of 8 May 2023. The appointment of Dr Farrar was previously announced in December 2022. In this role Dr Farrar will oversee the Science Division, bringing together the best experts and networks in science and innovation from around the world to guide, develop and deliver high quality health policies and services to the people who need them most. Prior to joining WHO Dr Farrar was Director of the Wellcome Trust.
translations.hypothesis: This new leadership body will work in collaboration with the WHO and national health agencies. New members of the leadership body include Dr. Jeremy Farrar, who will be the WHO's Chief Scientist, effective November 8, 2023. Dr. Farrar's appointment was announced in 2022. In this role, Dr. Farrar will lead the scientific community, fostering collaboration between leading scientists and organizations, and promoting innovation in global health. He will also be responsible for developing and implementing high-impact health programs for those most in need. Before joining the WHO, Dr. Farrar was the Director of the Wellcome Trust.

translations.source: Ngaphambi kokujoyina Wellcome ngo-2013 uDkt Farrar uchithe iminyaka engu-17 njengoMqondisi weClinical Reaserch Unit esiBhadlela seTropical Diseases eVietnam lapho ukucwaninga kwakhe kwakugxile kwezempilo emhlabeni ngokugxila ekuveleni kwezifo ezithelelanayo. UDkt Farrar ungumcwaningi wezokwelapha neziqu zobudokotela kanye nePHD. UDkt Ailan Li uzoba nguMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weUniversal Health Coverage,Healthier Populations kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Li izobheka imizamo yeNhlangano ukugqugquzela ukubangcono kwezempilo nokuphila ngokungenelela okuhlobene nezendawo ezenhlalakahle, ezempilo zokubhekene nezomnotho okufaka ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukulawula ugwayi, ukuphepha kwamakhemikhali, ukuphepha komgwaqo, izinhlelo zokudla nokunomsoco, ukusebenza siqu, ukungcoliswa komoya nokwemisebe yelana ngendlela ye-One Health. UDkt Li ubelokhu esebenza njengomele iWHO eKingdom of Cambodia kusukela ngomhlaka-15 kuNtulikazi 2019.
translations.reference: Before joining Wellcome in 2013 Dr Farrar spent 17 years as Director of the Clinical Research Unit at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases in Vietnam where his research interests were in global health with a focus on emerging infectious diseases. Dr Farrar is a clinician researcher with a medical degree and a PhD. Dr Ailan Li will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations as of 8 May 2023. In this role Dr Li will oversee the Organization’s efforts to promote better health and well-being through interventions relating to the environmental, social, and economic determinants of health, including climate change, tobacco control, chemical safety, road safety, food systems and nutrition, physical activity, air pollution and radiation, through a One Health approach. Dr Li has been serving as the WHO Representative to the Kingdom of Cambodia since 15 July 2019.
translations.hypothesis: Before joining Wellcome in 2013, Dr. Farrar spent 17 years as the Director of the Clinical Research Unit at the Institute of Tropical Diseases in Vietnam, where his research focused on global health challenges, particularly the prevention and control of infectious diseases. Dr. Farrar holds a PhD in public health and a medical degree. Dr. Ailan Li will be appointed as Deputy Director of Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations, starting on November 8, 2023. In this role, Dr. Li will oversee the organization's efforts to promote better health and well-being through initiatives related to sustainable development, health systems strengthening, and addressing social determinants of health, including climate change, food security, and access to clean water and sanitation. Dr. Li has also worked as a technical officer for the WHO in the Kingdom of Cambodia since November 15, 2019.

translations.source: Ngaphambi kwalesisikhundla, uDkt Li wasebenza njenge Regional Emergency Director weWHO Health Emergencies Programme eHhovisi leSifunda leWHO eWestern Pacific. UDkt Li uneziqu zobudokotela neziqu kwezesayensi yenhlalakahle yezempilo. UDkt Yukiko Nakatani uzoba nguMszi woMqondisi Jikelele wokungena kwezemithi nokukhiqizwa nemikhiqizo yezempilo. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Nakatani uzobheka ukuthuthuka nokwenza kwemigomo yeWHO nezinqubomgomo ukuqinisekisa ukutola ukulingana kumithi efanele, imigomo kanye nokuxilongwa kusona sonke isibalo sabantu yonke indawo okufaka ukuvikela nokuphendula ku-ephidemikhi. UDKt Nakatani ususebenze njengoMqondisi woMdlavuza nedivishini yokulawula izifo eJapanese uNgqongqoshe weZmpilo,ezokusebenza nenhlalakahle njengoTechnica Advisory kuMinister of State wezokusebenza zokugoma iCOVID-19 eJapan.
translations.reference: Prior to this role, Dr Li served as Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme in the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr Li holds a medical degree and degree in health social sciences. Dr Yukiko Nakatani will become Assistant Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health Products as of 2 May 2023. In this role Dr Nakatani will oversee the development and implementation of WHO’s norms and policies to ensure equitable access to quality medicines, vaccines and diagnostics for all populations everywhere, including for preventing and responding to epidemics. Dr Nakatani has been serving as the Director of the Cancer and Disease Control Division of the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare and as the Technical Advisor to the Minister of State for the COVID-19 Vaccination Task Force in Japan.
translations.hypothesis: Prior to this appointment, Dr. Li served as the Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme at the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr. Li holds a doctorate and a master's degree in public health. Dr. Yukiko Nakatani will serve as the Director General of the Department of Medical and Health Products. In this role, Dr. Nakatani will oversee the development and implementation of WHO guidelines and policies to ensure equitable access to essential medicines, health products, and services for all populations, including vulnerable groups. Dr. Nakatani has also served as a Director of the National Institute of Infectious Diseases in Japan and as a technical advisor to the Minister of State for Health, Labour and Welfare on the COVID-19 response in Japan.

translations.source: UDkt Nakatani uneziqu zobudokotela nePhD kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Razia Pendse uzoba nguChef deCabinet kusukela ngomhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Pendse uzohola iHhovisi loMqondisi Jikelele, esiza ukuhambisa iNhlangano kokuhamba phambili nemizamo, futhi uzoqinisekisa ukuhambisana neqembu lobuholi beWHO kuwona womathathu amazinga eWHO. UDkt Pendse ubesebenza njengoMqondisi weHealthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases kuHhovisi leSifunda leWHO eSouth-East Asia futhi wayemele iWHO eSri Lanka. UDkt Pendse uneziqu zobudokotela neziqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi.
translations.reference: Dr Nakatani holds a medical degree and a PhD in public health. Dr Razia Pendse will become the Chef de Cabinet as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Pendse will head the Director-General’s Office, helping to drive the Organization’s priorities and initiatives, and will ensure alignment within the WHO leadership team and across the three levels of WHO. Dr Pendse has been serving as the Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases in the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and was previously the WHO Representative to Sri Lanka. Dr Pendse holds a medical degree and master's degree in public health.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Nakatani holds a doctorate and a PhD in public health. Dr. Razia Pendse will assume the role of Deputy Director starting November 4, 2023. In this position, Dr. Pendse will lead the Office of the Director-General, providing support to the Director-General in strategic planning and implementation, and ensuring coordination with the WHO's three-level governance structure. Dr. Pendse previously served as Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases at the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia, and she has also worked for the WHO in Sri Lanka. Dr. Pendse holds a medical degree and a master's degree in public health.

translations.source: UDkt Jerome Salomon uzoba Ngumsizi waMqondisi Jikelele wezokukhavwa kweZempilo eMhlabeni, izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nokungaxhunyanwa ngazo kusukela ngomhlaka-17 Mbasa 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Salamon uzobheka iphothifoliyo enkulu yezinhlelo zezobuchwepheshe ezibheka iHIV, igciwane lehephathithisi, ukuthelelana kwezifo zocansi, isifo sofuba, umalalaeveva, izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe, ezempilo zengqondo, isifo sokusebenzisa izidakamizwa izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo njengezifo zenhliziyo, ushukena, izifo zondendende zokuphefumua kanye nomdlavuza. UDkt Salomon ubesebenza njengoMqondisi Jikelele weZempilo kubungqongqoshe beZempilo nokuvikela eFrance. Wayeyilungu leBhodi labaphathi beWHO ngaphambi kokuqokwa kwakhe futhi nesipiliyoni sakhe esikhulu ekuphathweni kwezinhlelo zempilo,izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nezempilo yomphakathi kumazwe angaphandle. UDkt Salomon uneziqu zobudokotela,izqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi kanye nePHD ku-ephidemiloji.
translations.reference: Dr Jérôme Salomon will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Communicable and Noncommunicable Diseases as of 17 April 2023. In this role Dr Salomon will oversee a broad portfolio of technical programmes covering HIV, viral hepatitis, sexually-transmitted infections, tuberculosis, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, mental health, substance use disorders, and noncommunicable diseases such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases and cancer. Dr Salomon has been serving as the Director-General for Health at the Ministry of Health and Prevention of France. He was a Member of the WHO Executive Board prior to his appointment and has extensive experience in health systems management, communicable diseases and international public health. Dr Salomon holds a medical degree, a master’s degree in public health and a PhD in epidemiology.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Jerome Salomon will serve as the Director of the World Health Organization's Global Programme on HIV/AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, effective from 17 March 2023. In this role, Dr. Salomon will oversee a large portfolio of programmes focused on HIV, tuberculosis, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, reproductive health, non-communicable diseases, mental health, and the prevention and treatment of vaccine-preventable diseases. Dr. Salomon has extensive experience in public health and international development, having previously served as the Director-General of the French Ministry of Health and as a member of the WHO's Executive Board. He also has a strong background in programme management, disease control, and public health policy. Dr. Salomon holds a doctorate in public health, a master's degree in epidemiology, and a PhD in infectious diseases.

translations.source: Lamaphothifolo amalungu akhona eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu: uDkt Samira Asma uzoqhubeka njengoMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weMininingo, ukuhlaziya nokulethwa kwemithelela. UDkt Asma ubheke iphothifoliyo eqinisekisa ukuthi idatha yezempilo ikhona futhi iyatholakala, futhi isetshenziswa ukwenzangcono imiphumela yezempilo emhlabeni wonke, okufaka ukulandelela nokuqhuba ukuqhubeka okubheke kokuqondiwe okungamabhiliyoni amathathu futhi okuhlobene nesempilo yeSustainable Development Goals. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO ngo-2018, uDkt Asma ubambe izikhundla zobuholi e-U.S. Centers for Disease Contrel and Prevention (CDC) iminyaka engaphezu kwamashumi amabili. Uneziqu zobudokotela kwezokuhlinza amazinyo kanye neziqu kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Bruce Alyward useqokelwe ukuba nguMsizi aMqondisi Jikelele weze-Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division kusuka ngomhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023.
translations.reference: The portfolios of existing members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Samira Asma will continue as the Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. Dr Asma oversees a portfolio which ensures that health data are reliable and accessible, and are used to improve health outcomes worldwide, including tracking and accelerating progress towards the triple billion targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining WHO in 2018, Dr Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She holds a doctorate degree in dental surgery and a master's degree in public health. Dr Bruce Aylward has been appointed Assistant Director-General of the Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division as of 4 May 2023.
translations.hypothesis: The new director-general of the World Health Organization (WHO) is Dr. Samira Asma, who will continue in her role as Deputy Director-General for Programs and Delivery. Dr. Asma will lead the effort to ensure that health data is available and accessible, and that it is used to improve health outcomes worldwide. Before joining the WHO in 2018, Dr. Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She has a doctorate in infectious diseases and a master's degree in public health.

translations.source: UDkt Aylward ubehola iTransformation Agenda yeWHO kanye nomsebenzi wenhlangano kuma-ejensi amaningi okuthola amathuluzi eCOVID-19 okuqhubeka kuhabhu (ACT-A). Kundima yakhe, uDkt Aylward uzohambisa ajenda yeNhlangano yokuguqula ezokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala njengemaphakathi lezokukhavwa kwezempilo emhlabeni kanye nokubheka umsebenzi weWHO wokusebenza kwezinhlelo zempilo, ukugoma, ukuzala, abakhulelwe kanye nezempilo yezingane. UDkt Alyward uneziqu zobudokotela kanye nemastazi yezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Hanan Balykhy uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMszi waMqondisi Jikelele weAntimicrobial Resistance. UDkt Balkhy uzonikezela ngobuchwepheshe nobuholi bezepolitiki ukubheka ezempilo nomthwalo wezomnotho ekungavumini kwesidakamizwa,okufaka ngokubambisana kwemikhakha eminingi neNhlangano yeZolimo noKudla Inhlangano yoMhlaba yezeMpilo yeZilwane kanye neUnited Nations Environment Programme. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO, uDkt Balkhy ubenguMqondisi omkhulu weWokuvikela ukuthelelana nokulawula eSaudi Arabia Ministry of Nationa Guard.
translations.reference: Dr Aylward has been leading WHO’s Transformation Agenda and the Organization’s work on the multi-agency Access to COVID-19 Tools Accelerator (ACT-A) Hub. In his new role, Dr Aylward will drive the Organization’s agenda to transform primary health care as central to universal health coverage, as well as overseeing WHO’s work on health systems, immunization and reproductive, maternal and child health. Dr Aylward holds a medical degree and a master’s degree in public health.Dr Hanan Balkhy will continue as Assistant Director-General for Antimicrobial Resistance. Dr Balkhy provides technical and political leadership to curb the health and economic burdens of drug resistance, including through multi-sectoral collaboration with the Food and Agriculture Organization, the World Organization for Animal Health, and the United Nations Environment Programme. Prior to joining WHO, Dr Balkhy was Executive Director for Infection Prevention and Control at Saudi Arabia’s Ministry of National Guard.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Aylward has been appointed to the WHO's Transformation Agenda and is also serving on the advisory board of the Access to COVID-19 Tools ACT-Accelerator. In this role, Dr. Aylward will lead the WHO's efforts to promote equitable access to essential health technologies, including vaccines, diagnostics, and therapeutics. Dr. Aylward is a medical doctor and public health specialist. Dr. Hanan Balykhy will continue to serve as the Director of the Antimicrobial Resistance Programme. Dr. Balykhy will bring his expertise and leadership to the WHO's work on antimicrobial resistance, including collaboration with a wide range of stakeholders, including the WHO, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, and the United Nations Environment Programme. Before joining the WHO, Dr. Balykhy was the Director of the National Guard's Counterterrorism and Security Department in Saudi Arabia.

translations.source: UDkt Balkhy uneziqu zobudokotela nokugxila ezifweni ezithelelanayo ezinganeni. UDkt Catharina Boehme uzoba nguMsizi kaMqondisi Jikelele weExternal Relations and Government kusukela mhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Boehme uzohola ukuphathwa kwamasu kweWHO ezindaweni zokuphatha,ukuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza nobudlelwano bokubambisana. Iphothifoliyo yakhe izofaka ubucayi bohlelo lweMember State, njengokuxoxisana kwabo kusivumelwano sobhubhane, ukwenza kabusha ukubusa kweWHO nokwenza kweziphakamiso ekugcinekeni kwezezimali. UDkt Boehme ubelokhu esebenza njengoMqondisi Jikelele weChef de Cabinet ngaleso sikhathi wahambisa ubuholi kumbono wesu,eqinisekisa ukuhambisana kuzona zonke izinhlangano namaMember State nababambisani.
translations.reference: Dr Balkhy holds a medical degree with specialization in paediatric infectious diseases. Dr Catharina Boehme will become Assistant Director-General, External Relations and Governance as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Boehme will lead WHO’s strategic engagement in the areas of governance, resource mobilization and partner relations. Her portfolio will include critical Member States processes, such as their negotiation of a pandemic accord, the reform of WHO’s governance and the implementation of recommendations on sustainable financing. Dr Boehme has been serving as the Director-General’s Chef de Cabinet during which time she drove the leadership’s strategic vision, ensuring alignment across the Organization and with Member States and partners.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Balkhy holds a doctorate in public health with a specialization in global health. Dr. Catharina Boehme will serve as Deputy Director of the External Relations and Government Affairs department, effective November 4, 2023. In this role, Dr. Boehme will lead the WHO's engagement with member states and other partners, including the development and implementation of strategic initiatives. Her portfolio will include overseeing the implementation of the Member State Action Plan, including discussions on the implementation of the Global Strategy on Health and Development, the revitalization of the WHO's normative functions, and the development of sustainable financing mechanisms. Dr. Boehme has previously served as Deputy Director-General of the WHO, where she oversaw the organization's external relations and partnerships.

translations.source: Ujoyine iWHO ngo-2021, ubelokhu enguChief Executive Officer weFIND, owezobudlelwane bamazwe angaphandle kwezokuxilonga. UDkt Boehme uneziqu zobudokotela nediploma ekuphatheni nezempilo yomphakathi. UDKt chikwe Ihekweazu uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMszi waMqondisi Jikelele weDivision of Health Emergency Interlligence and Survillance Systems kuHlelo lwezokuohuthumayo. Njengengxenye yephothifoliyo yakhe, uDkt Ihekweazu uhola iHabhu yeWHO yePandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, ukuhlanganisa ndawonye ababambisani ukubhekana nobhubhane lwekusasa nezingcuphe ze-ephidemikhi ngokungena kangcono kumininingo amandla okuhlaziya namathuluzi okuthatha izinqumo. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO uDkT Ihekweazu waba nguMqondisi Jikelele wokuqala weNigeria Centre fo Disease Control NCDC) kusuka ngo-2016 kuya ku-2021.
translations.reference: She joined WHO in 2021, having been the Chief Executive Officer of FIND, the international alliance for diagnostics. Dr Boehme holds a medical degree and diplomas in public health and management. Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu will continue as Assistant Director-General for the Division of Health Emergency Intelligence and Surveillance Systems in the Emergencies Programme. As part of his portfolio, Dr Ihekweazu leads the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, bringing together partners to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, analytical capacities, and tools for decision-making. Prior to joining WHO, Dr Ihekweazu was the first Director General of the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), from 2016 to 2021.
translations.hypothesis: He joined the WHO in 2021, and is currently the Chief Executive Officer of FIND (The Foundation for Innovative New Diagnostics) and a global leader in diagnostics. Dr. Boehme holds a doctorate and a diploma in public health. He is also a member of the Executive Board of the Division of Health Emergency Intelligence and Surveillance Systems of the WHO. As part of his portfolio, Dr. Ihekweazu leads the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, bringing together experts to improve pandemic preparedness and response through enhanced data analysis and decision-making tools. Before joining the WHO, Dr. Ihekweazu was the first Director-General of the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) from 2016 to 2021.

translations.source: UDkt Ihekweazu uneziqu zobudokotela ngokugxila ku-ephidomoloji yezifo ezithelelanayo kanye neziqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Michael Ryan uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMqondisi omkhulu woHlelo lweZiphuthumayo zeMpilo kuWHO. UDkt Ryan ubephethe ezokuphuthumayo zezempilo kuWHO eminyakeni engu-25 eyedlule futhi usanda kuhola ukuphendula kweziphuthumayo kweWHO ngokobhubhane lweCOVID-19. Uhola ukuphendula kweWHO ekubhedukeni kwezifo izinkinga zabantu kanye nokunye ukuphuthuma kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Ryan uyilungu elingumsunguli leGlobal Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN) esisize ukuphendula kumakhulu okubheduka kwezifo emhlabeni wonke.
translations.reference: Dr Ihekweazu holds a medical degree, with specialization as an infectious disease epidemiologist, and a master’s degree in public health. Dr Michael Ryan will continue as Executive Director of WHO’s Health Emergencies Programme. Dr Ryan has been managing health emergencies in WHO for the past 25 years and most recently led WHO’s emergency response to the COVID-19 pandemic. He leads WHO’s response to disease outbreaks, humanitarian crises and other public health emergencies. Dr Ryan is a founding member of the Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN), which has aided the response to hundreds of disease outbreaks around the world.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Ihekweazu holds a doctorate in epidemiology and public health, with a focus on infectious diseases. Dr. Michael Ryan will continue to serve as the Executive Director of the WHO's Department of Emergency Communications. Dr. Ryan has over 25 years of experience in emergency health at the WHO, and he recently led the WHO's response to the COVID-19 pandemic. He has also led the WHO's response to a number of other health emergencies, including outbreaks of Ebola and Zika. Dr. Ryan is a member of the Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN), which helps to coordinate the global response to outbreaks of infectious diseases.

translations.source: UDkt Ryan uneziqu kwezobudokotela kanye neziqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi. UMnumzane Raul Thomas uzoqhubeka njengoMsizi waMqondisi Jikelele weZokusebenza zamaBhizinisi. UMnumzane Thomas unomsebenzi wonke wokusebenza kwebhizinisi leWHO, okufaka uhlelomali, ezezimali, ezokusebenza zabantu, ukuphatha, ukucwaninga, ukuphatha ingcuphe, ukuzibophezela nokulandela kanye nokuphatha okujwayelekile. Umsebenzi wakhe ukwenza imizamo eminingi ngaphansi koguquko lwenhlangano nokwenza indawo yokusebenza ehloniphekayo. Ngesikhathi seminyaka yakhe engu-20 esebenza neWHO uMnumzane Thomas usesebenze kuzifunda zase-Afrika, e-Americas kanye nase-Eastern Mediterranean neWestern Pacific kanye ne-International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC).
translations.reference: Dr Ryan holds a degree in medicine and a master’s degree in public health. Mr Raul Thomas will continue as Assistant Director-General for Business Operations. Mr Thomas has overall responsibility for WHO’s business functions, including budget, finance, human resources, administration, audit, risk management, accountability and compliance, and general management. He is responsible for implementing many of the initiatives under the Organization’s transformation agenda and creating a respectful, inclusive workplace. During his 20 years of employment with WHO, Mr Thomas has served in the regions of Africa, the Americas, the Eastern Mediterranean and the Western Pacific, as well as with the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC).
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Ryan holds a doctorate and a master's degree in public health. Mr. Raul Thomas will continue in his role as Director of Business Operations for the WHO. Mr. Thomas has extensive experience in business operations for the WHO, including finance, human resources, project management, research, procurement, and general administration. His role is to lead and support various initiatives within the organization and to create a positive and productive work environment. During his 20 years of service at the WHO, Mr. Thomas has worked in Africa, the Americas, the Eastern Mediterranean, and the Western Pacific, as well as with the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC).

translations.source: UMnumzane Thomas uneziqu ze-master ekuphathweni kwenhlangano kanye neziqu ze-bachelor ekuphathweni kwebhizinisi.
translations.reference: Mr Thomas holds a master’s degree in organizational management and a bachelor’s degree in business administration.
translations.hypothesis: Mr. Thomas holds a master's degree in organizational management and a bachelor's degree in business administration.

translations.source: Umbiko womhlangano weWHO Technical Advisory Group ekwenziweni komgomo weCOVID-19 (TAG-CO-VAC) obubanjwe ngo-16-17 kuNdasa 2023 NgoNhlangulana 2022, iTAG-CO-VAC yashicilela isitatimende sesikhashana siveza imigomo encike kugciiwane lokuqala yaqhubeka yaqinisekisa amazinga aphezulu okuviikelwa okubhekene nezifo ezibucayi ezidalwa yiSARS-CoV-2 yonke ukwahlukana kokukhathazeka (amsVOC) Okufaka Omicron. Kepha ngokunikwa ububanzi besidalasihlungu nokungaqinisekisi ngokuqhubeka kokuguquka kwegciwane iTAG-CO-VAC ibone ukuthi kujwayelekile ukuthi ukusebenza kwemigomo encike kugciwane lokuqala ingehlisa emva kwesikhathi. I-TAG-CO-VAC ngakho yeluleke abakhiqizi bomgomo nabasemagunyeni abalawulayo ukuthi bacabange ukubuyekezwa kokwakhiwa kwesidalasihlungu somgomo okufaka i-Omicron, njengoba bahlukile ngokwesidalasihlungu SARS-CoV-2 ukwahlukana okwamanje, ekuphathweni komgomo wokwengeza. Abakhiqizi abaningi bomgomo sebedevelophe imigomo yeCOVID-19 ngokwenza isidalasihlungu esibuyekeziwe,lokhu kufaka kuncike ku-mRNA yebivalent embalwa ebitholakala ekuqaleni kweOmicrom ukulandelana kozalo, ukwengeza kugciwane lokuqala (lokhu kusho ukuthi, igciwane lenkomba + BA.1 noma BA.4/5) esigunyaziwe kokuphuthumayo ngabasemagunyeni abalalwulayo.
translations.reference: Report of the meeting of the WHO Technical Advisory Group on COVID-19 Vaccine Composition (TAG-CO-VAC) held on 16-17 March 2023 In June 2022, the TAG-CO-VAC published an interim statement highlighting that index virus-based vaccines continued to confer high levels of protection against severe disease caused by all SARS-CoV-2 Variants of Concern (VOCs), including Omicron. However, given the antigenic distance and uncertainties of further viral evolution, the TAG-CO-VAC recognized that it was likely that the effectiveness of vaccines based on the index virus would reduce over time. The TAG-CO-VAC therefore advised vaccine manufacturers and regulatory authorities to consider an update of vaccine antigen composition by including Omicron, as the most antigenically distinct SARS-CoV-2 variant thus far, for administration as a booster dose. Multiple vaccine manufacturers have developed COVID-19 vaccines with an updated antigenic composition; this includes several bivalent mRNA-based vaccines containing earlier Omicron descendent lineages, in addition to the index virus (i.e., index virus + BA.1 or BA.4/5), which have been authorized for emergency use by regulatory authorities.
translations.hypothesis: The statement from the WHO Technical Advisory Group on COVID-19 Vaccine (TAG-CO-VAC), issued on 16-17 November 2023, indicated that TAG-CO-VAC had concluded that the initial vaccine strategy should continue to prioritize high levels of protection against all variants of SARS-CoV-2, including Omicron. However, in light of the evolving nature of the virus and the uncertainty surrounding future variants, TAG-CO-VAC recommended that manufacturers and regulatory agencies consider a review of the initial vaccine strategy, including the potential for incorporating a variant-adapted vaccine that targets Omicron. Many vaccine manufacturers have already begun developing COVID-19 vaccines that incorporate a revised variant, such as a bivalent mRNA vaccine that includes the original strain and the Omicron BA.1 or BA.4/5 variants.

translations.source: Ngomhlaka-16 -17 kuNdasa 2023, iTAG-CO-VAC yaphinde yahlanganisa kuMuscat, e-Oman. Inhloso yalomhlangano ihlukene kabili: ukubheka ubufakazi bokwenza obubuyekeziwe kwemigomo yeCOVID-19 ehlanganisa ukulandelana kozalo kwe-Omicron njengesengeso somgomo; futhi ukwakha izikhathi zokwakhiwa komgomo weCOVID-19 kuziphakamiso ku-2023. Ubufakazi obubuyekezwe yi-TAG-CO-VAC ukuze buhlole ukusebenza kwemithi yokugomela i-COVID-19 ebuyekeziwe ehlanganise izinhlu zozalo ze-Omicron zifakiwe:
translations.reference: On 16-17 March 2023, the TAG-CO-VAC reconvened in Muscat, Oman. The purpose of the meeting was two-fold: to review the evidence on the performance of updated COVID-19 vaccines that incorporate descendent lineages of Omicron as a booster dose; and to establish timelines for COVID-19 vaccine composition recommendations in 2023. The evidence reviewed by the TAG-CO-VAC to assess the performance of updated COVID-19 vaccines that incorporated descendent lineages of Omicron included:
translations.hypothesis: On November 16-17, 2023, TAG-CO-VAC also included Muscat, Oman, in its discussions. The purpose of this meeting was twofold: to assess the feasibility of implementing updated COVID-19 guidelines, including adherence to Omicron variants, as a framework; and to develop a framework for COVID-19 vaccination strategies for 2023. The updated guidelines from TAG-CO-VAC focused on evaluating the effectiveness of updated COVID-19 vaccines, including those that incorporate Omicron variants:

translations.source: (1) izifundo zokutholakele kwe-ephidemoloji eshicilelwe ilinganisa okuyikho ngqo nokuhlobene nokusebenza komgomo kweBA.1 noma iBA.4/5 enemigomo yebivalent mRNA esebenza njengesikalo sokwengeza okubhekene nezimpawu nezifo ezibucayi (2) imininingo encike kulebhu kubukhulu nobubanzi bezimpendulo zokusebenza okubhekene nokudlule kokuzungeza kokwahlukana kweSARS-CoV-2 okwehliswa BA.1 noma iBA.4/5 imigomo ene-mRNA uma uqhathanisa nemigomo encike kugciwane lokuqala esetshenziswa njegesikalo sokwengeza (3) izifundo ezisekelwe elabhorethri kanye nedatha yokuqaphela ezimpendulo zenkumbulo yokuzivikela komzimba ukuze kuhlolwe umthelela wokuchayeka okuphindaphindiwe kwe-antigen ekugomeni nasekuvikelweni okubangelwa umgomo. Imininingwane eyengeziwe kubufakazi obubhekiwe yiTAG-CO-VAC bungatholakala ephepheni elihambisanayo. Ngokubhekiwe kwedatha echaziwe ngasenhla, iTAG-CO-VAC iyaphetha:
translations.reference: (1) published observational epidemiological studies of estimates of absolute and relative vaccine effectiveness of BA.1- or BA.4/5-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines used as a booster dose against symptomatic and severe disease; (2) laboratory-based data on the magnitude and breadth of cross-reactive immune responses against previous and circulating SARS-CoV-2 variants induced by BA.1- or BA.4/5- containing mRNA vaccines, as compared to index virus-based vaccines, used as a booster dose; and (3) laboratory-based studies and observational data on immune memory responses to evaluate the impact of repeated antigen exposure on vaccine-induced immunity and protection. Further details on the evidence reviewed by the TAG-CO-VAC can be found in the accompanying annex. Based on the review of the data described above, the TAG-CO-VAC concludes:
translations.hypothesis: (1) Epidemiological studies published in the journal that directly address and relate to the performance of the BA.1 or BA.4/5 mRNA vaccine, which is designed to elicit a specific immune response against specific viruses and diseases (2) The efficacy of the vaccine depends on the size and breadth of the immune response elicited by the BA.1 or BA.4/5 mRNA vaccine, as compared to the immune response elicited by the original vaccine (3) Laboratory-based studies and observational data on the effectiveness of the vaccine in preventing reinfection have been analyzed to assess the durability of the immune response and its protective effect. Additional information on the vaccine, TAG-CO-VAC, can be found in the corresponding publication. Based on the data presented, TAG-CO-VAC appears to:

translations.source: Isikalo sokwengeza kwemigomo encike kugciwane lokuqala iyaqhubeka iqinisekisa amazinga aphezulu okuvikela okubhekene nobucayi bezifo nokufa okudalwa yikona konke ukuhlukana SARS-CoV-2 okufaka okwesikhashana kwe-Omicron ukulandelana kozalo. Ukuvikelwa kuzifo ezibucayi nezinkomba zokuthelelana kulaliswa ukuqala kwemigomo enike kugciwane neBA.1 noma iBA.4/5 enebivalent yemigomo ye-mRNA eehlayo emva kwesikhathi. Kepha ukuvikelwa kulezifo ezibucayi kuyagcineka ngaphezu kokuvikelwa zempawini zokutheleleka. Uma uqhathanisa nemgomo encike kugciwane lokuqala, izikalo zokwengeza zeBA.1 noma iBA4.5 imigomo enebivalent mRNA kunganyusa ukusebenza komgomo okubhekene nezimpawu zezifo ngesikhathi izifundo zenamba encane zihlola imiphumela ebucayi kukhombisa ukufana kwesilinganiso sokusebenza komgomo. Kokubili iBA.1 neBA4/5 yemigomo equkethe mRNA yebivalent yandisa ubukhulu nobukhulu obubanzi bezimpendulo zokusebenza yonke indawo kukwahlukana kweSATS-CoV-2 Ngesikhathi kusetshenziswe njengesikalo sokwengeza uma uqhathanisa nemigomo encike kugciwane lokuqala.
translations.reference: Booster doses of index virus-based vaccines continue to confer high levels of protection against severe disease and death caused by all SARS-CoV-2 variants, including contemporary Omicron descendent lineages. Protection from severe disease and symptomatic infection induced by index virus-based vaccines and BA.1- or BA.4/5-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines declines over time. However, protection from severe disease is maintained longer than protection from symptomatic infection. As compared to index virus-based vaccines, booster doses of BA.1- or BA.4/5-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines may modestly increase vaccine effectiveness against symptomatic disease, while the small number of studies assessing severe outcomes show similar estimates of vaccine effectiveness. Both BA.1- and BA.4/5-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines enhance the magnitude and elicit greater breadth of cross-reactive immune responses to SARS-CoV-2 variants when used as a booster dose, as compared to the index virus-based vaccines.
translations.hypothesis: The initial booster dose continues to provide high levels of protection against severe illness and death caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, including the recent Omicron variant and its subvariants. Protection against severe illness and death is maintained even after the initial booster dose and with BA.1 or BA.4/5, which are Omicron subvariants with an mRNA booster dose administered several months after the initial vaccination. However, protection against severe illness remains higher than protection against infection. When compared to the initial booster dose, BA.1 or BA.4/5 mRNA booster doses show improved performance in preventing severe illness, as demonstrated in small studies that show a similar level of efficacy. Both BA.1 and BA.4/5 mRNA booster doses increase the breadth and durability of the immune response, leading to a more comprehensive protection against SARS-CoV-2 variants.

translations.source: BA4/5 imigomo equkethe imRNA yebivalent ilalisa ukulinganisa okukhulu kuzivimbasihlungu okubhekene nokulandelana kozalo kwamanje kweOmicron (BQ.1, XBB.1) uma uqhathanisa nemigomo yeBA.1- equkethe ibivalent yemRNA uma isetshenziswe njengesikalo sokwengeza.
translations.reference: BA.4/5-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines induced higher neutralizing antibody titres against recent descendent lineages of Omicron (BQ.1, XBB.1) as compared to BA.1-containing bivalent mRNA vaccines, when used as a booster dose.
translations.hypothesis: BA4/5 is a vaccine design that incorporates a bivalent mRNA, aiming for a significant increase in protection against the currently circulating Omicron variants (BQ.1, XBB.1) when compared to BA.1-based vaccines, particularly when used as a booster.
